topics.title: Domino Incident Investigation - Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Domino Incident Investigation - Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Domino Incident (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> A **domino incident** is a multi-event accidental sequence in which a **primary unwanted event** (PUE) at one installation unit triggers one or more **secondary unwanted events** (SUEs) at neighbouring units through the propagation of physical effects — namely **thermal radiation, overpressure shock waves, projectile fragments, and toxic dispersion** — thereby escalating the overall consequences far beyond those of the initiating event.

**Domino Incident Investigation — Technique** is the **structured, forensically rigorous methodology** used to deconstruct such multi-event accident chains. It involves:

1. **Reconstructing** the chronology and spatial propagation of all events,
2. **Identifying** the *escalation vectors* that linked the events,
3. **Evaluating** the failure (or absence) of *safety barriers* at each transition node, and
4. **Determining** the *root, contributing, and rootless* causes at every link of the domino chain.

> [!NOTE] **Origin of the Term**
> The phrase **"domino effect"** was popularised in process safety by **Trevor Kletz** (1942–2013), the pioneer of inherent safety, who drew the analogy from mechanically falling dominoes to cascading failures in chemical/process plants.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP] **Analogy: The Highway Pile-up**
> Imagine a foggy morning on a six-lane expressway. A single car brakes suddenly (the **Primary Event**). The car behind cannot react in time and rear-ends it (a **Secondary Event**). The impact pushes vehicles sideways, blocking lanes, and a chain reaction develops: Car 2 hits Car 3 → Car 3 hits Car 4 → a truck jackknifes → a fuel tanker ruptures → fire breaks out (a **Tertiary Event**).
>
> In this analogy:
> - Each **collision** = an event in the domino chain.
> - The **mechanism of impact** (kinetic energy transfer) = the *escalation vector*.
> - The **distance between cars, fog, and brake quality** = the *barriers* that could have arrested the chain.
> - The **investigator's job** is to determine *why* the first brake happened, *why* each following car failed to avoid impact, and *which barrier* (brake, fog light, lane discipline, crash barrier) failed — for *every link* in the chain.

In an industrial context, a **small flange leak (PUE)** may ignite as a **jet fire**, which radiates heat onto a nearby LPG bullet (**SUE #1**), causing it to BLEVE (**SUE #2**), and the resulting **fireball** engulfs a diesel tank farm (**SUE #3**) — a classic domino sequence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PUE** | Primary Unwanted Event — the *initiating* event |
| **SUE** | Secondary Unwanted Event — an event triggered by PUE or another SUE |
| **Escalation Vector** | Physical phenomenon carrying damage from one unit to another |
| **Domino Threshold** | Minimum intensity of an escalation vector that causes escalation |
| **Safety Barrier** | Engineered, procedural, or emergency control meant to prevent escalation |
| **Counterfactual** | A "what if" scenario used to test hypothesis of causation |
| **MTO Analysis** | Man-Technology-Organisation — multi-level cause taxonomy |
| **Bow-Tie** | Cause–threat–consequence diagram for barrier analysis |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of the Domino Incident Investigation Technique

The technique is structured into **six sequential phases**, each producing a deliverable that feeds into the next.

### Phase 1 — Data Acquisition & Site Preservation
- Collect **process data** (PI&D, P&ID, operating logs, DCS/SIS historian trends, alarm logs, ESD logs).
- Secure the **incident scene** (photogrammetry, drone LiDAR, witness videos, CCTV footage).
- Recover **physical evidence** (failed components, valve positions, gasket remnants, soot patterns, blast craters).

### Phase 2 — Timeline Reconstruction (Chronology Mapping)
- Establish a **master event timeline** $T_0 \to T_1 \to T_2 \dots \to T_n$ to the second (preferably millisecond).
- Cross-correlate **DCS traces**, **witness testimony**, and **physical damage gradients** (e.g., soot depth decreasing radially).

### Phase 3 — Domino Chain Mapping (Topology Identification)
- Identify every event $E_i$ and every escalation vector $V_{ij}$ linking $E_i \to E_j$.
- Construct a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of the chain; nodes = events, edges = escalation vectors.

### Phase 4 — Escalation Vector Quantification
For each link, compute the magnitude of the propagated effect and compare it against **domino threshold criteria** (see §2.3).

### Phase 5 — Multi-Level Causal & Barrier Failure Analysis
- Apply **MTO analysis** (Man, Technology, Organisation) at each link.
- Apply **Bow-Tie analysis** to identify the *threats*, *top event*, *consequences*, and *failed barriers*.
- Conduct **Counterfactual Analysis**: "Would the chain have continued *if* the relief valve had lifted?" — answering this tests each causal hypothesis.

### Phase 6 — Recommendations Generation (Chain-Breaking Actions)
- Recommendations must target **each link** of the chain — the *initiating causes*, the *escalation vectors*, and the *barriers* that failed.
- Categorise as **Engineering (E)**, **Procedural (P)**, or **Organisational (O)**.

## 2.2 Why This Technique Is Used (Engineering Justification)

- A single-cause **Root Cause Analysis** (e.g., 5-Why) is *insufficient* for cascade events — it would miss the **propagation failures**.
- Domino investigations produce **layout-level recommendations** (e.g., increased inter-unit spacing) that single-cause RCA cannot generate.
- It satisfies **COMAH / OSHA PSM / Seveso-III** regulatory expectations for major hazard installations.

## 2.3 Escalation Vector Damage Criteria (Standard Threshold Values)

These threshold values are widely used in quantitative domino risk assessment (Cozzani et al., Coche et al., Gledhill & Lines):

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Damage Intensities — Domino Escalation Thresholds**

| Escalation Vector | Damage Level | Threshold Intensity | Domino Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Radiation** (steady) | Process equipment damage | $\geq 37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^2$ | Severe damage in 30 s |
| **Thermal Radiation** (steady) | Escalation possible | $\geq 15 \; \text{kW/m}^2$ | Onset of escalation zone |
| **Overpressure (VCE / BLEVE)** | Heavy structural damage | $\geq 0.20 \; \text{bar}$ | Domino escalation threshold |
| **Overpressure** | Light damage | $\geq 0.05 \; \text{bar}$ | Onset of escalation zone |
| **Projectile (Missile)** | Penetration of vessel shell | $E_k \geq 10^4 \; \text{J}$ | Possible escalation |
| **Toxic Cloud** | LC50 exposure (30 min) | $\text{IDLH level reached}$ | Toxic domino escalation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Equations

> [!NOTE] **Use `\vert` or `\mid` inside cells — never the raw pipe character.**

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q(x) = \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi x^{2}}$ | Point-source thermal radiation model (W/m²) |
| 2 | $q_{\text{solid}} = \varepsilon \,\sigma\,(T_{f}^{4} - T_{a}^{4})\,F_{12}$ | Solid-flame view-factor model (W/m²) |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} = \chi_{r}\,\dot{m}''\,A_{\text{pool}}\,\Delta H_{c}$ | Radiative heat release rate (W) |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{\text{ref}} = f\!\left(\dfrac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}\right)$ | TNT-equivalent overpressure correlation |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance (m/kg¹ᐟ³) |
| 6 | $P_{ij} = \Phi_i \cdot V_{ij} \cdot P_j$ | Domino escalation conditional probability |
| 7 | $L_{\text{separate}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi \, q_{\text{th}}}}$ | Safe separation distance (m) |
| 8 | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | Probit equation for damage estimation |
| 9 | $\Pr = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | Probability of damage from probit $Y$ |
| 10 | $\Delta P_s = P_a\!\left(\dfrac{P_g}{P_a}\right)^{2/7}\!\left(\dfrac{r_0}{r}\right)^{2}\!-\!P_a$ | Sachs-scaled blast overpressure |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Layout Design:** Determines inter-unit distances in new installations.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Provides the domino frequency for LOPA/SIL calculations.
- **Emergency Planning:** Defines credible worst-case scenarios for off-site emergency plans.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory under EU Seveso-III Directive, Indian Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, and OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119.

---

# 🔷 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Safe Separation Distance (Point-Source Model)

### Problem Statement
A naphtha atmospheric storage tank (Tank A) is on fire with a total **radiative power output** $Q_{\text{rad}} = 1500 \; \text{MW}$. Adjacent Tank B contains LPG. Using the **point-source thermal radiation model**, calculate:

**(i)** The heat flux received at the wall of Tank B located **80 m** from Tank A.
**(ii)** The **minimum safe separation distance** at which the domino escalation threshold of $q_{\text{th}} = 37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^2$ is just satisfied.
**(iii)** Whether domino escalation will occur at 80 m, and what the *margin of safety* is.

### Given Data
- $Q_{\text{rad}} = 1500 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W}$
- Threshold $q_{\text{th}} = 37.5 \times 10^{3} \; \text{W/m}^2$
- Actual separation $x_{1} = 80 \; \text{m}$

### Solution

#### (i) Heat flux at 80 m

We apply the **point-source radiation model**:

$$
q(x) = \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi \, x^{2}}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
q(80) = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^{6}}{4 \pi \times (80)^{2}}
$$

Compute the denominator step by step:

$$
4 \pi (80)^{2} = 4 \times 3.14159 \times 6400 = 80424.77 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
q(80) = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{8.0425 \times 10^{4}} = 18651 \; \text{W/m}^{2} = 18.65 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation** — $18.65 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$ lies in the **"escalation possible"** band ($\geq 15 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$) but is **below** the equipment-damage threshold of $37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$. **Domino escalation is therefore credible but not certain** — escalation will occur if exposed for long enough (e.g., delayed water spray cooling fails).

#### (ii) Minimum safe separation distance

Rearrange the point-source equation for $x$:

$$
L_{\text{sep}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi \, q_{\text{th}}}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
L_{\text{sep}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{4 \pi \times 3.75 \times 10^{4}}}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
4 \pi \times 3.75 \times 10^{4} = 4 \times 3.14159 \times 37500 = 471238.9 \; \text{W}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L_{\text{sep}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{4.7124 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{3183.1} = 56.42 \; \text{m}
$$

#### (iii) Margin of Safety at 80 m

$$
\text{Margin} = \dfrac{q(80) - q_{\text{th}}}{q_{\text{th}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{18651 - 37500}{37500} \times 100\% = -50.3\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Verdict** — A **negative margin of -50.3 %** means the actual received flux is only ~50 % of the escalation threshold, so equipment damage is **not immediate** but escalation becomes possible if fire continues > 30 min without intervention.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — Overpressure Domino Threshold (TNT-Equivalent Method)

### Problem Statement
A vapour cloud explosion (VCE) occurs equivalent to $W_{\text{TNT}} = 5000 \; \text{kg}$. Determine the distance at which:
**(a)** 50 % probability of fatality occurs ($Z_{50} = 7.0 \; \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$),
**(b)** Heavy structural damage (domino threshold) occurs ($Z_{\text{dom}} = 5.6 \; \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$).

### Solution

The **scaled distance** is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = Z \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}
$$

Compute $W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = (5000)^{1/3} = 17.10 \; \text{kg}^{1/3}
$$

**(a) 50 % fatality distance:**

$$
R_{50} = 7.0 \times 17.10 = 119.70 \; \text{m}
$$

**(b) Heavy structural damage (domino) distance:**

$$
R_{\text{dom}} = 5.6 \times 17.10 = 95.76 \; \text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation** — Any equipment unit located **within 95.76 m** of the VCE has a high probability of being damaged so severely that it may release its inventory and act as the **next link** in the domino chain. Investigating this incident therefore requires inspecting all units within a ~100 m radius for secondary involvement.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #3 — Domino Escalation Probability Calculation

### Problem Statement
The conditional probability of escalation from unit $i$ to unit $j$ in a domino chain is modelled as:

$$
P_{ij} = \Phi_i \cdot V_{ij} \cdot P_j
$$

where:
- $\Phi_i = 0.6$ (probability that unit $i$ suffers catastrophic failure),
- $V_{ij} = 0.75$ (probability that the escalation vector successfully propagates from $i$ to $j$),
- $P_j = 0.4$ (conditional probability of unit $j$ failing once the escalation vector arrives).

Calculate the **overall probability of domino escalation** between these two units.

### Solution

Substituting:

$$
P_{ij} = 0.6 \times 0.75 \times 0.4 = 0.18
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
P_{ij} = 18\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Decision** — A **18 % probability** of escalation between two adjacent units is *not acceptable* for major hazard installations; the typical corporate acceptance threshold is **$P < 10^{-4}$/year**. This signals the need for **interposing additional barriers** (fireproofing, increased separation, water deluge).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🔷 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Domino Incident Investigation Methodology Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **six-phase domino investigation technique** as discussed in §2.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Data Acquisition and Site Preservation] --> A2[Phase 2: Timeline Reconstruction to Second Precision]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Domino Chain Mapping via Directed Acyclic Graph]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Escalation Vector Quantification]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: MTO and Bow-Tie Barrier Failure Analysis]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Chain Breaking Recommendations]
    A6 --> A7{Recommendations at Each Link}
    A7 --> B1[Engineering Controls E]
    A7 --> B2[Procedural Controls P]
    A7 --> B3[Organisational Controls O]
    A1 -.evidence.-> C1[DCS Historian Logs]
    A1 -.evidence.-> C2[Witness Statements]
    A1 -.evidence.-> C3[Physical Forensics]
    A5 -.tools.-> D1[MTO Taxonomy]
    A5 -.tools.-> D2[Bow-Tie Barriers]
    A5 -.tools.-> D3[Counterfactual Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram** — Solid arrows denote the **sequential workflow**; dashed arrows denote *supporting inputs* that feed into the phase from external sources.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Canonical Domino Chain (Topological Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[PUE: Flange Leak] -- Gas Cloud Dispersion --> E2[SUE 1: Vapour Cloud Explosion]
    E2 -- Blast Overpressure --> E3[SUE 2: Adjacent LPG Bullet Rupture]
    E3 -- BLEVE Fireball --> E4[SUE 3: Distant Diesel Tank Damage]
    E4 -- Pool Fire Thermal Radiation --> E5[SUE 4: Control Room Glazing Failure]
    E5 -- Escalation --> E6[Final Consequence: Major Loss]
    subgraph VectorLegend [Escalation Vectors Used]
        V1[Gas Dispersion]
        V2[Overpressure]
        V3[Fireball Thermal]
        V4[Pool Fire Radiation]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight** — Each event $E_i$ becomes the *parent* of the next event $E_{i+1}$. An investigator must examine **every edge** (escalation vector) for barrier failures; breaking *any single edge* halts the chain. This is the cardinal principle of **chain-breaking recommendations**.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Investigation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[DCS and SIS Data]
        I2[Physical Forensics]
        I3[Witness Accounts]
        I4[Process Documentation]
    end
    subgraph Engine
        N1[Event Identifier]
        N2[Vector Classifier]
        N3[Barrier Evaluator]
        N4[Root Cause Miner]
        N5[Recommendation Generator]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Domino Chain Map]
        O2[Failure Report]
        O3[Action Plan E P O]
        O4[QRA Update]
    end
    I1 --> N1
    I2 --> N1
    I3 --> N1
    I4 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> O1
    N5 --> O2
    N5 --> O3
    N5 --> O4
```

> [!TIP] **Engineering Reading** — This block diagram is the **functional architecture** of a Domino Investigation Toolkit: raw inputs feed an analysis engine (five sequential processing nodes), producing four downstream deliverables that feed back into the plant's **lifecycle risk management** loop.

---

# 🔷 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks in domino incident questions by:
> 1. Treating it as a *single-cause* incident and applying only 5-Why analysis.
> 2. Ignoring **escalation vectors** and discussing only the initiating event.
> 3. Confusing **domino threshold** values across thermal, pressure, and projectile vectors.
> 4. Omitting **counterfactual analysis** and therefore missing barrier failures.
> 5. Forgetting to provide **recommendations at every link** of the chain.
>
> Always address: **Event → Vector → Barrier → Cause → Recommendation** — for **each link**.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **Define the term "domino incident" as applied to process safety. List the four primary escalation vectors that drive such incidents.**

**Model Answer:**

A **domino incident** is a multi-event accidental sequence in which the consequences of a *primary unwanted event* (PUE) at one unit propagate through one or more escalation vectors to neighbouring units, triggering *secondary unwanted events* (SUEs) whose combined consequences exceed the original event. The four primary escalation vectors are:

1. **Thermal Radiation** — from pool fires, jet fires, fireballs.
2. **Overpressure Shock Waves** — from VCE, BLEVE, mechanical explosions.
3. **Projectile / Missile Fragments** — from vessel ruptures, BLEVEs, BLEVE-rocketing.
4. **Toxic Gas Dispersion** — from chlorine, ammonia, hydrogen fluoride releases.

*(Word count: 75 — sufficient for a 3-mark answer)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **State the standard domino-escalation threshold values for (i) thermal radiation on process equipment and (ii) overpressure from a vapour cloud explosion. Mention their engineering significance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Vector | Threshold Intensity | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Radiation | $37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$ | Onset of severe equipment damage in ~30 s; used as the basis for inter-unit safe separation |
| Overpressure | $0.20 \; \text{bar}$ | Heavy structural damage threshold; the basis for blast-wall sizing and QRA damage radii |

These thresholds are used to compute **domino escalation frequencies**, to size **firewalls / blast walls**, and to define **exclusion radii** in QRA studies under Seveso-III / Indian MSIHC Rules.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU ESE convention: each 14-mark question carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Solve both sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **six-phase methodology of domino incident investigation**, illustrating with a suitable flowchart. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A kerosene pool fire has a radiative power output of $Q_{\text{rad}} = 800 \; \text{MW}$. An adjacent ammonia storage vessel is located **60 m** away. Using the point-source thermal radiation model, determine whether domino escalation will occur if the threshold is $37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$. Also calculate the **minimum safe separation distance**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Methodology of Domino Incident Investigation**

| Phase | Activity | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Data Acquisition | Gather DCS historian, witness statements, physical evidence, P&IDs | Evidence file |
| 2. Timeline Reconstruction | Build master timeline to second precision | Master chronology |
| 3. Domino Chain Mapping | Construct DAG of events and vectors | Chain topology map |
| 4. Vector Quantification | Compute heat flux, overpressure, missile energies at each link | Quantitative escalation matrix |
| 5. MTO + Bow-Tie + Counterfactual | Identify barrier failures and root causes | Causal report |
| 6. Recommendations | Propose chain-breaking actions categorised E / P / O | Action plan |

*(Marks distribution: Phase description 4 marks + flowchart representation 2 marks + role of each deliverable 1 mark = 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $Q_{\text{rad}} = 800 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W}$, $x_{1} = 60 \; \text{m}$, $q_{\text{th}} = 37.5 \times 10^{3} \; \text{W/m}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Compute received heat flux at 60 m:**

$$
q(60) = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{4 \pi \times (60)^{2}} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 3.14159 \times 3600} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{45238.93}
$$

$$
q(60) = 17683 \; \text{W/m}^{2} \approx 17.68 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with threshold:**

$$
17.68 \; \text{kW/m}^{2} < 37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}
$$

> **Verdict:** Received flux is below the damage threshold, so **immediate domino escalation is NOT expected**. However, since $17.68 \;\text{kW/m}^{2} > 15 \;\text{kW/m}^{2}$, the vessel lies in the *escalation possible* zone — escalation is **credible** if fire persists for a long time without cooling intervention.

**Step 3 — Minimum safe separation distance:**

$$
L_{\text{sep}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{4 \pi \times 3.75 \times 10^{4}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{8}}{4.7124 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{1697.65} = 41.20 \; \text{m}
$$

> **Result:** $L_{\text{sep}} = 41.20 \; \text{m}$. Since actual distance (60 m) > required distance (41.20 m), the layout is **safe** with respect to thermal radiation — but protective measures (deluge cooling, fireproofing) are still recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Discuss the **Bow-Tie analysis** method as applied to domino incident investigation. Explain its advantages over a simple 5-Why approach. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A VCE equivalent to $W_{\text{TNT}} = 15000 \; \text{kg}$ occurs at a refinery. Estimate the distance at which: (i) 1 % fatality probability exists, (ii) 50 % fatality probability exists, (iii) Heavy structural damage threshold is exceeded. Use the standard scaled-distance values. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bow-Tie Analysis in Domino Investigation**

A **Bow-Tie diagram** is a *cause-consequence* chart with the *Top Event* (the domino escalation node) at its centre. The **left wing** lists *threats* (PUE causes), the **right wing** lists *consequences* (SUE outcomes), and the **central knot** is protected by **preventive barriers** (left) and **mitigative barriers** (right).

**Structure Applied to Domino Investigation:**

| Side | Elements | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Left (Threats) | Initiating causes | Corrosion, overfilling, hot work, lightning strike |
| Centre (Top Event) | Escalation node | LPG vessel failure due to fire impingement |
| Right (Consequences) | Downstream events | BLEVE → fireball → neighbouring tank damage |

**Advantages over 5-Why:**

1. **Visualises multiple barriers simultaneously**, whereas 5-Why traces only one causal line.
2. **Maps both preventive AND mitigative** controls in a single diagram.
3. **Reveals 'barrier decay'** — which degraded barriers allowed escalation to proceed.
4. **Supports audit** of safety-critical elements in a structured way.
5. **Aligns directly with the risk graph** required for ISO 31000 / IEC 61511 SIL assessments.

*(Marks distribution: Bow-tie structure 3 marks + application to domino 2 marks + 2 advantages with examples 2 marks = 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $W_{\text{TNT}} = 15000 \; \text{kg}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = (15000)^{1/3} = 24.66 \; \text{kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply scaled-distance thresholds:**

| Probability Level | Z (m/kg¹ᐟ³) | Calculation | Distance R (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 % fatality | 13.5 | $13.5 \times 24.66$ | **333.0 m** |
| 50 % fatality | 7.0 | $7.0 \times 24.66$ | **172.6 m** |
| Heavy structural damage | 5.6 | $5.6 \times 24.66$ | **138.1 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Decision** — All process equipment within ~138 m of the VCE must be inspected for *secondary involvement*. Emergency planning must assume un-controlled releases from any such unit. Plant layout upgrade should target a separation of **$\geq$ 333 m** from the VCE epicentre to achieve fatality risk $< 1\%$.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **Important Things to Remember**

- **Domino incident = PUE + ≥1 SUE + propagation via escalation vector** — never confuse with a single-cause accident.
- The **four escalation vectors** are: *thermal radiation, overpressure, projectiles, toxic dispersion*.
- **Key domino thresholds:** $37.5 \; \text{kW/m}^{2}$ (thermal) and $0.20 \; \text{bar}$ (overpressure) — memorise these for KTU exams.
- The technique has **six phases**: Data Acquisition → Timeline → Chain Mapping → Vector Quantification → Causal/Barrier Analysis → Recommendations.
- **Every recommendation must target a specific link** — *chain-breaking* is the central aim.
- **Point-source radiation model:** $q(x) = \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi x^{2}}$.
- **Safe separation distance:** $L_{\text{sep}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi q_{\text{th}}}}$.
- **TNT-equivalent scaling:** $R = Z \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$; remember $W^{1/3}$ scaling is *cube-root*, not linear.
- **Bow-Tie analysis** is preferred over 5-Why for domino investigation because it visualises multiple barriers.
- **Counterfactual analysis** ("what if barrier X had worked?") is the test of causal necessity.
- **MTO taxonomy** (Man-Technology-Organisation) ensures *all* causal layers are investigated, not just the immediate operator error.
- **Threshold values must be quoted in SI units** in the KTU answer sheet — never use $\text{cal/cm}^{2}$ or non-standard units.
- The technique satisfies **Seveso-III / OSHA PSM / Indian MSIHC Rules** regulatory expectations — useful in 14-mark answers to demonstrate *contextual* understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logic Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Logic Diagram** in safety engineering is a deductive graphical-analytical tool that represents the **logical combinations of elementary failures, human errors, and external events** that can lead to an undesired top-level event (e.g., a major accident, hazardous release, or system failure). The most common forms in KTU-aligned safety management modules are:

- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — top-down deductive logic structure.
- **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** — bottom-up inductive logic structure.
- **Bow-Tie Diagram** — combined cause–consequence logic representation.

It employs **Boolean algebra** where basic events are connected through logic gates (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `Voting/N`) to express the causal pathways of accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT751 – Module 1):** Logic Diagrams are the *backbone* of probabilistic safety assessment (PSA) and are universally applied in process industries (oil & gas, petrochemical, nuclear), aerospace, and occupational safety audits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Logic Diagram as a **medical diagnosis flowchart**. Imagine a patient arrives with symptoms (headache, fever, nausea). A doctor uses **AND/OR logic** to combine these symptoms into possible diseases. Similarly, in safety engineering, the engineer plays the "doctor" — the **Top Event** is the "disease," and the **basic events** (pump failure, valve closed, operator error) are the "symptoms." The Logic Diagram tells you which combination of symptoms causes the disease.

A second analogy: **A Logic Diagram is like a detective's logic puzzle.**
- **Top Event:** "The crime occurred."
- **Intermediate Events:** "Weapon was used" OR "Forced entry occurred."
- **Basic Events:** "Trigger pulled," "Door broken."
- Only when *all* AND-gate inputs occur (or *any* OR-gate input occurs) does the higher event happen.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | λ | **failures / hour** |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | **hours** |
| Mean Time To Repair | MTTR | **hours** |
| Unavailability (top event) | Q(t) | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| Probability of failure on demand | PFD | **dimensionless** |
| Common-cause failure factor | β | **0.01 – 0.10 (typical β-factor)** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE papers, students must correctly differentiate **AND logic** (all events must occur simultaneously) from **OR logic** (any one event is sufficient). Confusing these two is the single most common reason for mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of a Logic Diagram (Step-by-Step Logic)

A Fault Tree Logic Diagram is built using three hierarchical layers:

1. **Top Event (TE):** The undesired event being analyzed (e.g., "Refinery fire").
2. **Intermediate Events (IE):** Logical combinations of lower events (sub-failures) that propagate toward the top.
3. **Basic Events (BE):** The lowest-level, independent failure events (pump fails, valve stuck, sensor fails).

#### 2.1.1 Logic Gates — The Engine of the Diagram

| Gate | Symbol | Boolean Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| AND | ⊗ | $T = A \cdot B$ | Top event occurs **only if ALL** inputs occur |
| OR | ⊕ | $T = A + B$ | Top event occurs **if ANY** input occurs |
| NOT | (bar) | $T = \overline{A}$ | Top event occurs **if input does NOT** occur |
| Voting k/N | ⌈k/n⌉ | $T$ occurs if **at least k** of n inputs occur |

#### 2.1.2 Why and How of Each Step

- **Why AND?** Used when multiple *independent* failures must *co-occur* (e.g., valve fails to close AND pressure sensor fails to alarm).
- **Why OR?** Used when *any one of several* parallel pathways leads to failure (e.g., pump A fails OR pump B fails OR pump C fails).
- **How?** Each event is quantified by its probability $P_i$; the gate then mathematically combines the probabilities using the appropriate Boolean rule.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AND-gate top probability (independent events) | $P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Events are **statistically independent** |
| 2 | OR-gate top probability (independent events) | $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | Events are **statistically independent** |
| 3 | Approx. OR-gate (rare events) | $P_{\text{OR}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Valid only when $P_i \ll 1$ |
| 4 | NOT-gate | $P(\overline{A}) = 1 - P(A)$ | Complementary event |
| 5 | Minimal Cut Set probability | $P_{\text{CS}} = \prod_{j \in \text{CS}} P_j$ | Multiply AND-gate events in cut set |
| 6 | System unavailability | $Q(t) \approx \lambda \cdot \text{PFD}$ | Used in IEC 61508 SIL calc |
| 7 | Failure rate → unavailability | $Q(t) \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | For exponential distribution |
| 8 | Exponentials – MTBF relation | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Constant failure rate |
| 9 | 2/3 voting gate | $P_T = P_1 P_2 (1-P_3) + P_1(1-P_2)P_3 + (1-P_1)P_2 P_3 + P_1 P_2 P_3$ | All combinations with $\geq 2$ successes |
| 10 | Boolean expansion for OR→AND | $A + BC = (A+B)(A+C)$ | De Morgan distributive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Trick:** In KTU 2024 numericals, when two or more events have **very small probabilities** ($P \leq 0.1$), the approximation $P_{\text{OR}} \approx \sum P_i$ is acceptable. But if any $P_i > 0.1$, you **must** use the exact formula $1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Logic Diagrams are used in:

- **Process Safety Management (PSM):** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 mandates hazard analysis (HAZOP, FTA).
- **Nuclear Industry:** Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) for reactor safety.
- **IEC 61508/61511:** Determination of **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 to 4)**.
- **Aerospace:** Failure Mode, Effects & Criticality Analysis (FMECA).
- **KTU Industrial Training:** Students are expected to apply logic diagrams in plant safety audits during their B.Tech projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — OR-Gate Probability from First Principles

Let $T$ be the OR-top event with inputs $A$, $B$, $C$ that are **independent**.

The probability of the *complement* of $T$ (i.e., the top event **does not** occur) is the event where **all** inputs fail to occur:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\overline{T}) &= P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B} \cap \overline{C}) \\
&= P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{B}) \cdot P(\overline{C}) \quad \text{(independence)} \\
&= (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)(1 - P_C)
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the complement rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1 - P(\overline{T}) \\
&= 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)(1 - P_C) \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generalisation for $n$ independent events:**

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{OR}}(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — AND-Gate Probability from First Principles

For an AND-top event $T = A \cdot B \cdot C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(A \cap B \cap C) \\
&= P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C) \quad \text{(independence)} \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{AND}}(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rare-Event Approximation for OR-Gate

Starting from the exact OR-gate expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{OR}}(T) &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i) \\
&= 1 - \left[ 1 - \sum_i P_i + \sum_{i<j} P_i P_j - \cdots \right] \\
&= \sum_i P_i - \sum_{i<j} P_i P_j + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

When each $P_i \ll 1$ (typically $P_i < 0.1$), all product-of-products terms are negligible:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{OR}}(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \quad \text{when } P_i \ll 1}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A logic diagram has an OR-gate with 3 inputs:
- Pump A failure: $P_A = 0.02$
- Pump B failure: $P_B = 0.03$
- Pump C failure: $P_C = 0.04$

Compute (a) the exact top-event probability, and (b) the rare-event approximation.

**Solution:**

(a) Exact calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.04) \\
&= 1 - (0.98)(0.97)(0.96) \\
&= 1 - 0.912576 \\
&= 0.087424
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) Rare-event approximation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &\approx 0.02 + 0.03 + 0.04 \\
&= 0.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** The approximation error is approximately $(0.09 - 0.087424)/0.087424 \approx 2.95\%$, which is acceptable for $P_i < 0.1$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Gate Selection

| Scenario Type | Gate Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Two safety valves both required to close a line | AND | Both must function |
| Three parallel pumps, any one can supply water | OR (k/n = 1/3) | Single success is sufficient |
| Fire requires fuel AND oxygen AND ignition source | AND | All three necessary |
| Multiple independent ignition sources can start fire | OR | Any source is enough |
| Voting 2-out-of-3 sensors | Voting 2/3 | At least 2 must vote for trip |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Process Plant System]
    A0 --> A1[Hazard Identification HAZOP]
    A1 --> A2[Define Top Undesired Event]
    A2 --> A3[Construct Logic Diagram]
    A3 --> A4[Identify Basic Events]
    A4 --> A5[Apply Boolean Algebra]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Minimal Cut Sets]
    A6 --> A7[Quantify Probabilities]
    A7 --> A8[Importance Measures]
    A8 --> A9[Risk Evaluation and SIL Determination]
    A9 --> A10[Recommendations and Safety Improvements]
```

### 4.2 Fault Tree Logic Diagram — Worked Example (Pump & Valve System)

The system fails if **either** the pump fails **OR** the valve fails to close **AND** the pressure sensor fails. Represented as:

$$
T = P_{\text{pump}} + (P_{\text{valve}} \cdot P_{\text{sensor}})
$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TE[TOP EVENT: System Failure]
    OR1[OR Gate]
    AND1[AND Gate]
    BE1[BE1: Pump Failure]
    BE2[BE2: Valve Fails to Close]
    BE3[BE3: Pressure Sensor Fails]
    TE --> OR1
    OR1 --> BE1
    OR1 --> AND1
    AND1 --> BE2
    AND1 --> BE3
```

### 4.3 Event Tree Logic Diagram — After-Initiating-Event Branching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IE[INITIATING EVENT: Pipe Rupture]
    N1[Detection System Works?]
    N2[Isolation Valve Closes?]
    N3[Emergency Cooling Active?]
    O1[S1: Safe Shutdown]
    O2[S2: Minor Leak, Contained]
    O3[S3: Toxic Release Limited]
    O4[S4: Major Accident]
    IE --> N1
    N1 -- Yes --> N2
    N1 -- No --> N3
    N2 -- Yes --> N3
    N2 -- No --> O3
    N3 -- Yes --> O1
    N3 -- No --> O4
```

### 4.4 Bow-Tie Diagram Structure (Cause–Consequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[HAZARD: Hydrocarbon Release]
    T[TOP EVENT: Loss of Containment]
    C1[Consequence 1: Fire]
    C2[Consequence 2: Explosion]
    C3[Consequence 3: Toxic Exposure]
    P1[Preventive Barrier 1: Detection]
    P2[Preventive Barrier 2: Isolation]
    M1[Mitigative Barrier 1: Fire Water System]
    M2[Mitigative Barrier 2: Emergency Response]
    H --> P1 --> T
    T --> M1
    P2 --> T
    T --> M2
    T --> C1
    T --> C2
    T --> C3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a Logic Diagram as applied to safety engineering. List the principal logic gates used in fault tree construction.*

**Model Answer:**
A Logic Diagram is a deductive analytical tool that graphically represents the combinations of basic events (equipment failures, human errors, environmental conditions) leading to an undesired top event, using Boolean logic. Principal logic gates: **AND**, **OR**, **NOT**, and **Voting (k/n)**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Boolean expressions for AND and OR gates used in fault tree analysis. When is the rare-event approximation valid?*

**Model Answer:**
- AND: $T = A \cdot B$, probability $P_T = P_A \cdot P_B$.
- OR: $T = A + B$, probability $P_T = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)$.
- Rare-event approximation valid when each $P_i \ll 1$ (typically $P_i < 0.1$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with a suitable logic diagram how you would analyse the failure of a chemical reactor cooling system using Fault Tree Analysis. Identify the top event, intermediate events, and basic events.*

**Model Solution:**

**Top Event:** Reactor Overpressure → Vessel Rupture.

**Intermediate Events:**

$$
\text{TOP EVENT} = \text{IE1} + \text{IE2}
$$

where

$$
\text{IE1} = \text{Cooling Pump Failure (AND Coolant Valve Stuck)}
$$

$$
\text{IE2} = \text{Pressure Relief Valve Failure (AND Sensor Failure)}
$$

**Basic Events:**

| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| BE1 | Cooling pump motor trip |
| BE2 | Coolant isolation valve stuck |
| BE3 | PRV fails to open on demand |
| BE4 | Pressure transmitter fails low |
| BE5 | Operator fails to act on alarm |

**Logic structure:**

$$
T = (BE_1 \cdot BE_2) + (BE_3 \cdot BE_4) + (BE_1 \cdot BE_5)
$$

**Quantification:** Using AND rule $P = \prod P_i$ for each cut set, then OR over the three independent cut sets:

$$
P_T = P_C - \prod_{k=1}^{3}(1 - P_{\text{CS}_k})
$$

**Engineering recommendation:** Install redundant cooling pumps with auto-switchover and a SIL-2 rated independent pressure trip — both reduce cut-set probability by an order of magnitude.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Three independent safety barriers have failure probabilities $P_1 = 0.005$, $P_2 = 0.008$, $P_3 = 0.010$. They are arranged in (i) series (all must fail for top event) and (ii) parallel (any one failure causes top event). Calculate the top event probability for both arrangements and comment.*

**Model Solution:**

**Case (i): Series — failure occurs only if ALL three barriers fail.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T^{\text{series}} &= P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \\
&= 0.005 \times 0.008 \times 0.010 \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (ii): Parallel — any single failure causes top event.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T^{\text{parallel}} &= 1 - (1 - P_1)(1 - P_2)(1 - P_3) \\
&= 1 - (0.995)(0.992)(0.990) \\
&= 1 - 0.9771444 \\
&= 0.0228556
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Series configuration (AND-gate) is **highly desirable** for safety barriers because the failure probability drops drastically. This is the fundamental principle behind **redundancy in safety-instrumented systems (SIS)** — placing barriers in series (so all must fail) instead of parallel (where any one failure is catastrophic).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat event-tree diagram, analyse the sequence of events following a hydrocarbon leak in an offshore platform. Identify the success and failure pathways and compute the overall incident probability for given barrier reliabilities.*

**Model Solution:**

**Initiating Event:** Hydrocarbon Leak (frequency = $10^{-2}$/year).

**Barriers (in sequence):**

- B1: Gas Detection — reliability $R_1 = 0.99$
- B2: Automatic Shutdown (ESD) — reliability $R_2 = 0.98$
- B3: Fire-water Deluge — reliability $R_3 = 0.95$

**Event Tree Outcomes:**

| Path | B1 | B2 | B3 | Outcome | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Success | Success | Success | Safe | $0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9219$ |
| 2 | Success | Success | Fails | Minor fire | $0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.05 = 0.04851$ |
| 3 | Success | Fails | — | Major leak | $0.99 \times 0.02 = 0.0198$ |
| 4 | Fails | — | — | Major accident | $1 - 0.99 = 0.01$ |

(Note: Once B2 fails, B3 is irrelevant; conditional probability assumed = 1.)

**Overall incident probability (any unsafe outcome):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{incident}} &= 1 - P_{\text{safe}} \\
&= 1 - 0.9219 \\
&= 0.0781 \text{ per initiating event}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Annual risk:**

$$
R = f_{\text{init}} \times P_{\text{incident}} = 0.01 \times 0.0781 = 7.81 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /year}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Differentiate between Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA). Construct a 2/3 voting-gate logic diagram for a triplex sensor system and derive the top event probability expression.*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | FTA | ETA |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (bottom-up) |
| Question asked | "How can this top event occur?" | "What happens after this initiating event?" |
| Output | Boolean cut sets & failure pathways | Sequence of outcomes & frequencies |
| Application | Identify root causes | Assess consequences & barriers |

**2/3 Voting Gate Derivation:**

For three independent sensors with success probabilities $R_A$, $R_B$, $R_C$, the trip signal occurs when **at least 2 sensors agree**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{trip}) &= P(\geq 2 \text{ successes}) \\
&= P(AB\bar{C}) + P(A\bar{B}C) + P(\bar{A}BC) + P(ABC) \\
&= R_A R_B (1 - R_C) + R_A (1 - R_B) R_C + (1 - R_A) R_B R_C + R_A R_B R_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special case:** If $R_A = R_B = R_C = R$:

$$
P(\text{trip}) = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3 = 3R^2 - 3R^3 + R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3
$$

For $R = 0.95$:

$$
P(\text{trip}) = 3(0.95)^2 - 2(0.95)^3 = 3(0.9025) - 2(0.857375) = 2.7075 - 1.71475 = 0.99275
$$

This demonstrates that a **2/3 voting system provides high reliability (99.275%)** even with individual sensor reliability of only 95%, justifying its widespread use in IEC 61508 SIL-3 systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Mixing AND/OR formulas** — Students often write $P_{\text{OR}} = \prod P_i$ or $P_{\text{AND}} = \sum P_i$. *Deduct 1 mark immediately.*
> 2. **Forgetting complement** — In OR-gate problems, $1 - P_{\text{event}}$ is *not* the failure probability of the OR-gate; use the full complement product.
> 3. **Ignoring independence assumption** — Always state "events are statistically independent" before applying AND/OR formulas; otherwise, conditional probability is required.
> 4. **Wrong cut-set identification** — A cut set must contain events from a single AND chain; do not mix events from different branches of the tree.
> 5. **Voting gates misread** — For 2/3 voting, "at least 2 succeed" includes the all-3-succeed case.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ☐ Logic Diagram = deductive/inductive graphical tool using Boolean gates for accident causation modelling.
- ☐ **Fault Tree (FTA)** → top-down; **Event Tree (ETA)** → bottom-up; **Bow-Tie** → combined.
- ☐ AND-gate probability: $P_T = \prod P_i$ (independence assumed).
- ☐ OR-gate probability: $P_T = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$.
- ☐ Rare-event OR approximation: $P_T \approx \sum P_i$ **only when** $P_i < 0.1$.
- ☐ Minimal Cut Set = smallest set of basic events whose joint occurrence causes the top event.
- ☐ Voting k/n gate: $P_T = \sum \binom{n}{j \geq k} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$.
- ☐ Always state the **independence assumption** before any multiplication.
- ☐ Units: probabilities are dimensionless; failure rates in **failures/hour**; MTBF in **hours**.
- ☐ IEC 61508/61511 standards use logic diagrams for SIL determination.
- ☐ Boolean laws to remember: $A + AB = A$, $A(A+B) = A$, $\overline{A+B} = \bar{A} \cdot \bar{B}$ (De Morgan).
- ☐ Common β-factor for common-cause failures: $\beta = 0.01$–$0.10$.
- ☐ KTU 2024 expects both **qualitative** (cut sets) and **quantitative** (probabilities) treatment.
- ☐ Drawing clear gate symbols (AND = ⊗, OR = ⊕) earns full structural marks in board exams.
- ☐ Always end with an engineering recommendation (redundancy, SIL upgrade, procedural barrier).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Requirements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Input Requirements

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Safety Engineering and Management**, **Input Requirements** refer to the systematically identified, documented, and validated set of data, information, resources, regulatory mandates, technical parameters, and stakeholder expectations that must be gathered and processed *before* the design, implementation, or audit of any Safety Management System (SMS), Hazard Analysis study, or Risk Assessment framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *Input Requirements constitute the foundational data architecture upon which the entire safety engineering lifecycle is constructed. They form the boundary conditions for hazard identification (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA), risk quantification, and the formulation of control hierarchies mandated by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, ISO 45001:2018, and the KTU-referenced NEP 2020 Occupational Safety framework.*

### 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Input Requirements

Input Requirements are broadly classified into **six (6) functional tiers**, each serving a distinct cognitive and operational purpose within the safety engineering workflow:

| Tier | Input Class | Primary Function |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| T1 | **Regulatory & Statutory Inputs** | Legal compliance anchoring |
| T2 | **Process & Operational Inputs** | System boundary definition |
| T3 | **Hazard & Risk Data Inputs** | Hazard identification feeding |
| T4 | **Human & Ergonomic Inputs** | Human-factor modeling |
| T5 | **Environmental & Site Inputs** | External condition mapping |
| T6 | **Organizational & Resource Inputs** | Management system scaffolding |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Medical Diagnosis" Analogy:**
> Think of Input Requirements exactly like a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing any medicine, the doctor requires: **(i)** patient history (regulatory/operational past), **(ii)** current symptoms (hazard data), **(iii)** blood reports and X-rays (technical measurements), **(iv)** lifestyle habits (human factors), and **(v)** available hospital resources (organizational inputs). Without these inputs, any "safety prescription" is mere guesswork and is professionally, ethically, and legally indefensible.

In safety engineering terms, the equivalent of the doctor's prescription is the **Safety Case**, **Hazard Log**, or **Emergency Response Plan**, and the inputs are the diagnostic tests that justify every decision within it.

### 1.4 Critical Boundary Parameters

The following **standardized metrics** govern the validation of Input Requirements and must be documented for any KTU-mandated safety audit:

- **PHR (Process Hazard Rate):** $\geq 10^{-6}$ events/person-year (industry baseline)
- **ALARP Threshold:** Risk reduction cost $\geq$ **\$10,000 per statistical life saved** (UK HSE benchmark)
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** PFD range from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ (IEC 61508)
- **Acceptable Fatal Accident Rate (FAR):** $\leq 1.0$ per $10^{8}$ person-hours worked

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Five Pillars" Framework of Input Requirements

The systematic acquisition of Input Requirements follows a **Five-Pillar Logical Sequence** that mirrors the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle of ISO 45001:

### Pillar 1: **Regulatory Boundary Identification**

- **Why:** Every safety intervention must be legally defensible.
- **How:** The safety engineer must compile applicable statutes (Factories Act 1948, OSHA Standards, EPA Norms, IS 14489, NFPA Codes).
- **Output:** A *Legal Register* — a structured document mapping each clause to operational activity.

### Pillar 2: **Process & System Boundary Definition**

- **Why:** Hazards exist only relative to a defined system boundary.
- **How:** Establish **Node Boundaries** in P&ID diagrams, define **Material Flow Limits** (kg/s), **Pressure Envelopes** (Pa), and **Temperature Windows** (K).
- **Output:** A *System Boundary Document* with explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria.

### Pillar 3: **Hazard & Risk Data Aggregation**

- **Why:** Quantitative risk assessment (QRA) requires numerical failure data.
- **How:** Source data from **OREDA (Offshore REliability DAtabase)**, **IEEE Std 500**, **T-Book**, and **CCPS PERD**.
- **Output:** A *Failure Rate Database* normalized to $\lambda$ (failures per $10^{6}$ hours).

### Pillar 4: **Human Reliability & Behavioral Input**

- **Why:** 70–80% of industrial accidents have human-factor roots (Heinrich's Domino Theory).
- **How:** Apply **HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique)** or **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)**.
- **Output:** *Human Error Probability (HEP)* values typically in range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$.

### Pillar 5: **Resource & Organizational Capability Input**

- **Why:** Safety systems fail when organizational support is absent.
- **How:** Conduct a *Capability Maturity Assessment* (CMM Level 1–5).
- **Output:** A *Resource Gap Matrix* identifying budget, training, and tooling deficits.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas constitute the **exam-ready mathematical backbone** of Input Requirements quantification in safety engineering. All formulas are **SI-unit consistent**.

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Individual Risk | $IR = f \cdot p$ | $f$ = frequency (yr$^{-1}$), $p$ = probability of fatality | QRA baseline calculation |
| 2 | Societal Risk (F-N Curve) | $F \cdot N^{a} = C$ | $F$ = frequency, $N$ = number of fatalities, $a, C$ = constants | Population risk evaluation |
| 3 | Failure Rate (Exponential) | $\lambda(t) = \text{const}$ | failures / $10^{6}$ hrs | Component reliability input |
| 4 | Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD = \lambda \cdot T_{proof}/2$ | $T_{proof}$ = proof test interval (hrs) | SIL verification |
| 5 | Risk Score (Fine-Kinney) | $R = L \cdot E \cdot C$ | $L$ = likelihood, $E$ = exposure, $C$ = consequence | Hazard ranking input |
| 6 | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | $ALARP \Rightarrow \Delta R / \Delta C \le \beta$ | $\beta$ = disutility factor (\$/life) | Cost-benefit decision |
| 7 | Crowding Factor | $CF = N_{actual} / N_{design}$ | dimensionless | Occupancy input |
| 8 | Fatal Accident Rate | $FAR = \frac{N_{fatal}}{T_{hrs}} \cdot 10^{8}$ | persons / $10^{8}$ hrs | Benchmarking |
| 9 | Expected Loss | $EL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot c_i$ | currency or fatalities | Insurance / risk financing |
| 10 | Human Error Rate (HEART) | $HEP = K \cdot \prod G_i$ | $K$ = nominal error, $G_i$ = error producing conditions | Human reliability input |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These inputs are not academic exercises. They directly drive:

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Determining **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** Independent Protection Layers (IPLs).
- **Nuclear Engineering:** Defining **Defense-in-Depth** parameters for reactor licensing.
- **Construction Sector:** Calibrating **Fall Protection** anchor loads per ANSI Z359.
- **Manufacturing:** Establishing **Machine Guarding** requirements per OSHA 1910.212.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Individual Risk from Input Data

**Problem Context (KTU-Standard):**
A process plant operates a reactor containing **2,500 kg** of hydrocarbon at **25 bar** and **180°C**. Historical data (from OREDA-equivalent) provides a leak frequency of **$5 \times 10^{-4}$** per year for the primary gasket, and the probability of ignition given release is **0.08**. Compute the **Individual Risk (IR)** at the nearest occupied building located **50 m** away, given a **probit-based fatality probability** of 0.35 at that distance.

### Step 1: Identify Input Requirements

Gather all five tiers of input:

- **T2 (Process Input):** Mass $= 2500$ kg, Pressure $= 25 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- **T3 (Hazard Input):** $\lambda_{leak} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ yr$^{-1}$
- **T3 (Conditional Input):** $P(\text{ignition} \mid \text{release}) = 0.08$
- **T5 (Site Input):** Distance $d = 50$ m
- **T3 (Consequence Input):** $P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{ignition}, d=50) = 0.35$

### Step 2: Formulate the Event Tree

$$
\text{Leak} \rightarrow \text{Ignition} \rightarrow \text{Escalation to Fatality}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Multiplicative Risk Equation

The Individual Risk (IR) is computed as the joint probability of the entire fatal-event chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= \lambda_{leak} \times P(\text{ignition} \mid \text{release}) \times P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{ignition}, d) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= (5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1}) \times (0.08) \times (0.35) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Perform Intermediate Multiplications

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8 \times 10^{-2} \times 35 \times 10^{-2} \\
   &= 5 \times 8 \times 35 \times 10^{-4-2-2} \\
   &= 1400 \times 10^{-8} \\
   &= 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compare Against Acceptability Threshold

**KTU / UK HSE Reference Standard:** Maximum tolerable IR for workers $= 10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$; broadly acceptable $= 10^{-6}$ yr$^{-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR_{computed} &= 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
IR_{threshold} &= 10^{-6} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \, \text{(acceptable)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Conclusion via ALARP Test

Since $IR_{computed} > IR_{threshold}$, the risk falls in the **ALARP Region** (Tolerable only if residual risk is reduced *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*). Cost-benefit analysis is mandated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{ALARP} &= \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta R \cdot N_{exposed}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\beta \le \$10{,}000$/life saved, the mitigation is mandatory under KTU-2024 framework.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Inputs to Safety Outputs

| Input Tier | Specific Data | Processing Tool | Safety Output |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Regulatory | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 | Compliance Audit | Legal Register |
| Process | P&ID, Flow rates | HAZOP | Deviation Matrix |
| Hazard | $\lambda$ from OREDA | FMEA | RPN Score |
| Human | HEP from HEART | THERP | HRA Report |
| Environmental | Wind rose, Seismic zone | QRA | Consequence Map |
| Organizational | Budget, Manpower | CMM Assessment | Resource Plan |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

*Define the term "Input Requirements" as applied to the design of a Safety Management System. List any FOUR major categories of inputs.*

**Model Answer (Board-Standard 3-Mark Layout):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Input Requirements are the systematically identified set of regulatory, technical, operational, human, environmental, and organizational data and resources that must be compiled and validated prior to the execution of any hazard analysis or risk assessment within a Safety Management System.

**Four Major Categories (2 Marks – 0.5 each):**

1. **Regulatory & Statutory Inputs** — e.g., Factories Act, OSHA Standards, IS Codes.
2. **Process & Operational Inputs** — e.g., P&IDs, Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS).
3. **Hazard & Reliability Data Inputs** — e.g., OREDA failure rates, incident logs.
4. **Human & Ergonomic Inputs** — e.g., shift patterns, training records, HEP data.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

*Explain the role of "Failure Rate Data" as a critical Input Requirement in Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA). What is the standard reference database used in the petrochemical industry?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role (2 Marks):**
Failure rate data ($\lambda$, expressed in failures per $10^{6}$ hours) is the foundational numerical input for **Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA)**. It enables quantification of:
- Component unreliability $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$
- Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) for Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)
- Core Damage Frequency (CDF) in nuclear and process plant PRA models

Without validated $\lambda$ values, all subsequent QRA outputs (Individual Risk, Societal Risk, F-N curves) become analytically meaningless.

**Standard Reference (1 Mark):**
The **OREDA (Offshore REliability DAtabase)** project, jointly maintained by SINTEF and major offshore operators, is the industry-benchmark source. Supplementary sources include **IEEE Std 500**, **CCPS PERD**, and **T-Book (TNO)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In 14-mark Part B questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> (a) Writing generic definitions without **numerical quantification** (lose up to 4 marks).
> (b) Failing to **justify the choice** of input category with a real engineering example (lose 2 marks).
> (c) Skipping the **units and dimensional consistency** check on derived quantities (lose 2 marks).
> (d) Noting inputs but **omitting their interrelationships** in the safety decision chain (lose 3 marks).
> **Always structure your answer with: Definition → Classification → Justification → Worked Example → Conclusion.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a suitable block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of Input Requirements** for an Advanced Safety Engineering Management System in a chemical process plant. Enumerate all six input tiers with one representative data item per tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gasoline storage tank farm has a **primary catastrophic rupture frequency** of $1 \times 10^{-5}$ yr$^{-1}$. The probability of a **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** given rupture is **0.6**, and the probability of **fatality within 100 m radius** given VCE is **0.7**. The nearest control room is occupied by **4 personnel** for 24 hours/day, 365 days/year. Compute:
   (i) The **Individual Risk** at the control room location.
   (ii) The **Expected Annual Loss of Life (EALL)**.
   (iii) Determine whether this risk is **ALARP-compliant** given the UK HSE threshold of $10^{-6}$/yr. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) – 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Input Requirements constitute the structured set of regulatory, technical, human, environmental, and organizational data validated prior to safety system design.

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. Draw a six-tier block architecture in the answer script:
- Tier 1 → Regulatory (Factories Act, OSHA)
- Tier 2 → Process (P&ID, Flowrate)
- Tier 3 → Hazard Data ($\lambda$ from OREDA)
- Tier 4 → Human Factor (HEP from HEART)
- Tier 5 → Environmental (Wind rose, Seismic)
- Tier 6 → Organizational (Budget, Training)

**Justification with Example (2 Marks):**
In a naphtha hydrotreater unit, the six tiers feed into a **HAZOP workshop**, producing deviations like *"No flow"* or *"High temperature"*, which then propagate to **LOPA** analysis and **SIL verification**.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Without these six tiers, the resulting Safety Case is non-compliant with ISO 45001 Clause 4 (Context of the Organization).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) – 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_{rupture} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ yr$^{-1}$
- $P(\text{VCE} \mid \text{rupture}) = 0.6$
- $P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{VCE}, d=100\text{m}) = 0.7$
- $N_{personnel} = 4$
- Exposure fraction $= \frac{24 \times 365}{24 \times 365} = 1.0$ (full-time occupancy)

**Step (i): Individual Risk Calculation (3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= \lambda_{rupture} \times P(\text{VCE} \mid \text{rupture}) \times P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{VCE}, d) \\
   &= (1 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.6) \times (0.7) \\
   &= 4.2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Expected Annual Loss of Life (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EALL &= IR \times N_{personnel} \\
     &= (4.2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4 \\
     &= 1.68 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{fatalities/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): ALARP Test (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR_{computed} &= 4.2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
IR_{tolerable} &= 10^{-3} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \, \text{(UK HSE upper limit)} \\
IR_{acceptable} &= 10^{-6} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \, \text{(broadly acceptable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
Since $IR_{computed} = 4.2 \times 10^{-6}$ lies *between* the acceptable and tolerable thresholds, the risk is in the **ALARP Region**. The organization must demonstrate that further risk reduction is impracticable or grossly disproportionate in cost, otherwise mitigation (e.g., relocation of control room or blast wall installation) is mandatory.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **Human Reliability Inputs** in Safety Engineering. Apply the **HEART technique** to estimate the Human Error Probability for an operator tasked with manually closing a block valve during a chemical leak emergency, given that the task is performed under **severe time pressure** and **poor lighting**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ammonia refrigeration plant experiences **leak events** with frequency $3 \times 10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$. The conditional probability of **toxic exposure** is **0.45**, and the conditional probability of **fatality given toxic exposure** is **0.20**. The facility employs **10 workers** for **300 days/year, 8 hours/day**. Calculate:
   (i) The **Annual Expected Fatality Count**.
   (ii) The **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)**.
   (iii) Compare with the international benchmark of **2.0 fatalities per $10^{8}$ person-hours**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) – 7 Marks:**

**Importance of Human Reliability Inputs (2 Marks):**
- Heinrich's Pyramid indicates **70–80%** of industrial accidents involve human error.
- Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) inputs are critical for **PRA**, **SIL assignment**, and **emergency response validation**.
- HEART provides a structured, validated, and reproducible method for quantifying HEP.

**HEART Methodology (3 Marks):**

The HEART equation is:

$$
HEP = K \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} G_i
$$

Where:
- $K$ = nominal human error probability for the generic task
- $G_i$ = Error Producing Condition (EPC) multipliers

**Application (2 Marks):**
For "manual valve closure under emergency":

- Generic task: $K = 3 \times 10^{-2}$ (relatively unfamiliar, complex task)
- EPC 1 — Severe time pressure: $G_1 = 11$ (HEART Table)
- EPC 2 — Poor lighting: $G_2 = 1.8$ (HEART Table)

$$
\begin{aligned}
HEP &= (3 \times 10^{-2}) \times (11) \times (1.8) \\
    &= (3 \times 10^{-2}) \times 19.8 \\
    &= 5.94 \times 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
An HEP of $\approx 0.6$ indicates the operator has a **60% probability** of failing to close the valve correctly under these conditions — this is **unacceptable** and mandates engineered safeguards (e.g., remote-operated valves, ESD systems).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) – 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_{leak} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$
- $P(\text{toxic exposure} \mid \text{leak}) = 0.45$
- $P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{toxic exposure}) = 0.20$
- $N_{workers} = 10$
- Exposure time $= 300 \times 8 = 2400$ hrs/person/year

**Step (i): Annual Expected Fatality Count (3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{fatal event chain}) &= (3 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.45) \times (0.20) \\
&= 2.7 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{fatalities/year}] &= P(\text{fatal event}) \times N_{workers} \\
&= (2.7 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10 \\
&= 2.7 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{fatalities/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Fatal Accident Rate (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FAR &= \frac{N_{fatal}}{T_{hrs}} \times 10^{8} \\
&= \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 2400} \times 10^{8} \\
&= \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{4}} \times 10^{8} \\
&= 11.25 \, \text{fatalities per } 10^{8} \text{ person-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): Benchmark Comparison (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FAR_{computed} &= 11.25 \\
FAR_{benchmark} &= 2.0 \, \text{(international industry average)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
Since $FAR_{computed} = 11.25 \gg FAR_{benchmark} = 2.0$, the plant exhibits a fatality rate **5.6 times higher** than the international benchmark. This demands **immediate risk reduction** measures including: ammonia leak detection sensors, ventilation upgrades, SCBA availability, and emergency shutdown interlocks. The risk is **NOT ALARP-compliant** under any reasonable regulatory framework.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Input Requirements (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Input Requirements = the validated data foundation for safety system design.
- **Six Tiers:** Regulatory → Process → Hazard Data → Human → Environmental → Organizational.
- **Key Standards:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, ISO 45001:2018, IEC 61508, Factories Act 1948.
- **Critical Formulas:**
  - $IR = \lambda \times P_{conditional1} \times P_{conditional2} \times \ldots$
  - $HEP = K \times \prod G_i$ (HEART method)
  - $EALL = IR \times N_{exposed}$
  - $FAR = (N_{fatal}/T_{hrs}) \times 10^{8}$
- **Threshold Benchmarks:**
  - $IR_{acceptable} = 10^{-6}$ yr$^{-1}$
  - $IR_{tolerable} = 10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$
  - $FAR_{benchmark} = 2.0$ per $10^{8}$ person-hrs
  - $ALARP_{cost} \le \$10{,}000$/statistical life saved
- **Major Databases:** OREDA, IEEE Std 500, CCPS PERD, T-Book.
- **Common Methods:** HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA, LOPA, HEART, THERP.
- **Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  - Skipping units in numerical problems.
  - Ignoring the multiplicative nature of conditional probability chains.
  - Forgetting exposure fractions when computing FAR.
  - Failing to distinguish between **acceptable**, **tolerable**, and **intolerable** risk zones.
