# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Safety Engineering.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Safety Engineering is the engineering discipline that applies scientific and engineering principles to identify, evaluate, and control workplace hazards and risks. It integrates knowledge from mechanical, electrical, chemical, and civil engineering to design safer workplaces, equipment, processes, and systems. (3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for scope + 1 for integration aspect)

---

### Question 2: What is an Unsafe Act? Give two examples.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An Unsafe Act is a human behavioral deviation from accepted safe procedures that directly permits or causes an accident. (1 mark)
**Two Examples:** (2 marks — 1 mark each)
1. Operating machinery at unsafe speed without authorization.
2. Failure to use personal protective equipment (PPE) despite known requirement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Q(A)(a):** Explain in detail **Herbert William Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation. List all five factors in sequence with a suitable illustration. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) proposed that workplace injuries are not random events but the predictable outcome of a sequence of five interrelated factors. (1 mark for introduction)

**The Five Dominos in Sequence:**

| Domino | Factor | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D1** | Ancestry & Social Environment | Inherited traits, upbringing, neighborhood, education, socioeconomic status that shape the worker's character | 1 |
| **D2** | Worker Fault (Personal Defect) | Recklessness, nervousness, ignorance, or ill-health arising from D1 | 1 |
| **D3** | Unsafe Act + Unsafe Mechanical/Physical Condition | The immediate trigger — either human error or a hazardous physical condition | 1.5 |
| **D4** | The Accident | The unwanted release of energy causing visible damage | 1 |
| **D5** | The Injury | Damage to person or property | 1 |

**Critical Insight (1.5 marks):** The chain can be broken by removing any one of the dominos. Heinrich advocated that the most effective point of intervention is between D3 and D4, by introducing strong management supervision, engineering controls (guards, interlocks), and worker training — thus preventing the accident from ever resulting in injury.

---

**Q(A)(b):** A factory employs **180 workers** working an average of **48 hours/week for 50 weeks/year**. During the year, there were **8 recordable injuries**, of which **3 resulted in lost time**. The total lost workdays were **42 days**.
**Calculate:** (i) TRIR, (ii) LTIFR, and (iii) Severity Rate. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate total man-hours worked.

$$\text{Man-Hours} = 180 \times 48 \times 50 = 432{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate TRIR.

$$\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{8 \times 200{,}000}{432{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}600{,}000}{432{,}000} \approx 3.70 \text{ injuries per 100 FTE-years}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate LTIFR.

$$\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{6}}{432{,}000} = \dfrac{3{,}000{,}000}{432{,}000} \approx 6.94 \text{ lost-time injuries per million man-hours}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Severity Rate.

$$\text{SR} = \dfrac{42 \times 200{,}000}{432{,}000} = \dfrac{8{,}400{,}000}{432{,}000} \approx 19.44 \text{ lost days per 100 FTE-years}$$

**Mark Allocation:** 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 1.5 for Step 3, 1.5 for Step 4.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Q(B)(a):** Compare and contrast **Heinrich's Domino Theory** and **Frank Bird's Loss Causation Model**. Highlight at least **five points of difference**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | **Heinrich (1931)** | **Bird (1966)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size** | Based on ~75,000 incidents | Based on ~1.7 million incidents (297 companies) |
| 2 | **Focus** | Personal/individual causes | Management system causes |
| 3 | **Sequence Model** | Five linear dominos | Three-tier cause hierarchy (immediate → basic → root) |
| 4 | **Blame Tendency** | Implicitly blames worker (Domino 2) | Explicitly blames management ("Lack of Control") |
| 5 | **Heinrich's Ratio Update** | 1 : 29 : 300 (unchanged) | Refined to **1 : 10 : 30 : 600** (serious : lost-time : minor : no-injury) |
| 6 | **Modernity** | Historical/foundational | Modern/industry-preferred |
| 7 | **Application** | Basic awareness training | Comprehensive safety audits |

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for introduction + 6 × 1 mark = 6 marks for six clear comparison points.

---

**Q(B)(b):** Discuss the **James Reason Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation. How does it improve upon Heinrich's model? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Swiss Cheese Model, proposed by **James Reason in 1990**, views accidents as the result of **alignments of failures** across multiple defensive layers within a system, each represented as a slice of cheese containing holes (failures/latent conditions). (1 mark)

**Four Layers (1 mark each):**

1. **Organizational Influences** — Resource management, organizational culture, safety climate.
2. **Unsafe Supervision** — Inadequate supervision, planned inappropriate operations, failure to correct known problems.
3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** — Environmental factors, personnel factors, workplace conditions.
4. **Unsafe Acts (Last Layer)** — Errors and violations by frontline workers.

**How It Improves Upon Heinrich's Model (3 marks):**

1. **Multi-layered view:** Unlike Heinrich's linear chain, it recognizes multiple simultaneous defenses rather than a single causal sequence.
2. **Latent failures:** Reason explicitly identifies *latent* conditions (organizational and supervisory) that may lie dormant for years before combining with active failures to cause harm.
3. **Non-blaming:** It shifts the philosophical burden from the worker to the organization — accidents happen because the system fails, not because one person errs.
4. **Defense-in-depth logic:** It provides a clear engineering rationale for installing multiple independent safety barriers.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
> 
> 1. **Wrong constant in formulas:** Using 1,000,000 in TRIR instead of 200,000 loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Listing only 4 dominos in Heinrich's theory:** The fifth (Ancestry & Social Environment) is frequently forgotten. Examiner deducts 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Writing "Bird's Theory" without specifying "Loss Causation Model":** Generic name alone costs 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units in final answers:** Always state *per 100 FTE-years* or *per million man-hours*.
> 5. **Blurring immediate vs. basic causes:** In Bird's model, "substandard acts" are immediate causes, not basic causes. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark theory questions:** A neat labeled flowchart (even hand-drawn) earns 1 extra mark per KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Safety Engineering** = identification + evaluation + control of hazards.
- ✅ **The 5 Dominos (Heinrich):** Ancestry → Worker Fault → Unsafe Act/Condition → Accident → Injury.
- ✅ **The 3 Tiers (Bird):** Lack of Management Control → Basic Causes → Immediate Causes.
- ✅ **The 4 Layers (Reason):** Organizational → Supervisory → Preconditions → Unsafe Acts.
- ✅ **TRIR constant = 200,000** (US OSHA); **LTIFR constant = 1,000,000** (International).
- ✅ **Severity Rate numerator** = Lost Workdays (not number of incidents).
- ✅ **FSI** = TRIR × Severity Rate (dimensionless composite).
- ✅ **Heinrich's Ratio** = **1 : 29 : 300** (Major : Minor : No-injury).
- ✅ **Bird's refined ratio** = **1 : 10 : 30 : 600** (Serious : Lost-time : Minor : No-injury).
- ✅ **Always state units** with the final numerical answer to fetch full marks.
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram** in 7-mark theory questions (Domino sequence, Swiss Cheese slices, or management workflow).
- ✅ **The Swiss Cheese Model** is the modern "gold standard" used in ISO 45001 and high-reliability organizations.
- ✅ **Safety Management is a continuous PDCA loop** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — not a one-time project.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unavailability Analysis of Protective Systems - Technique
Reliability Analysis of Automatic Control Systems - PES Safety System Development, Logic Diagram, System Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reliability Analysis of Automatic Control Systems - PES Safety System Development, Logic Diagram, System Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Programmable Electronic System (PES) Safety System** refers to an automatic control system—based on programmable electronic devices (PLCs, DCS, smart sensors, microprocessors)—that is specifically engineered, validated, and deployed to bring an industrial process to a safe state when predetermined hazardous conditions are detected. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511**), a PES forms the core of the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** and implements one or more **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** with a quantified **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 4)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim intent):** > A PES Safety System is a computer-based system used in safety-critical applications to perform **continuous automatic control**, **trip logic**, and **interlock functions**, replacing or augmenting conventional relay-based and hardwired safety circuits. Its reliability must be mathematically proven through quantitative analysis. The key functional objectives of a PES safety system are: 1. **Detection** of a hazardous process deviation (sensor layer). 2. **Decision / Logic Solving** (logic solver: PLC, DCS, trip amplifier). 3. **Actuation** (final element: shutdown valve, circuit breaker, vent). 4. **Feedback verification** of the safe-state action. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Autopilot" Model Imagine a commercial airliner. The **autopilot** (PES) constantly monitors altitude, airspeed, and engine parameters. If the aircraft approaches a stall condition, the system does *not* ask the pilot for permission—it **automatically fires the recovery logic**, lowers the nose, and increases thrust. - The **angle-of-attack sensor** is like the PES's **input sensor**. - The **flight control computer** is the **logic solver**. - The **hydraulic actuators on the elevators** are the **final elements**. Just as the autopilot must be *demonstrably reliable* (failure rate ≤ 10⁻⁹ per flight hour for catastrophic functions), an industrial PES must satisfy **SIL-3 PFDavg ≤ 10⁻³** for high-consequence hazards such as reactor over-pressure protection. > [!NOTE] > **Why a "PES" and not a "Relay"?** > Relays are deterministic but bulky, hard to test online, and have a fixed failure mode. A PES allows **complex voting logic (1oo2, 2oo3)**, **self-diagnostics**, and **remote reconfiguration**, but introduces **software common-cause failure (CCF)** risks that demand systematic reliability analysis. ## 1.3 Metrics Introduced in This Topic | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Failure Rate** | $\lambda$ | failures/hour | Failures per unit time (assumed constant) | | **Mean Time To Failure** | **MTTF** | hours | For non-repairable items | | **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF** | hours | For repairable items | | **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | hours | Average downtime | | **Probability of Failure on Demand** | **PFD** | dimensionless | For low-demand mode | | **Probability of Failure per Hour** | **PFH** | per hour | For high-demand/continuous mode | | **Safe Failure Fraction** | **SFF** | % | Diagnostic coverage indicator | | **Safety Integrity Level** | **SIL** | 1 – 4 | Risk reduction target | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PES Safety Life-Cycle (Why this matters in KTU exams) The development of a PES safety system follows the **V-Model** codified in IEC 61508. The relevant phases for a KTU student are: 1. **Concept & Scope Definition** – Identify the Equipment Under Control (EUC) and the hazards. 2. **Hazard & Risk Analysis** – HAZOP / FMEA / LOPA to derive a target SIL. 3. **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** – Functional + Integrity requirements. 4. **Design & Engineering** – Architecture (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3), redundancy. 5. **Implementation** – Coding (ladder / FBD / structured text), SIL-rated PLC. 6. **Verification & Validation** – FMEDA, proof tests. 7. **Operation, Maintenance & Modification** – Proof test intervals, bypass procedures. 8. **Decommissioning** – Functional safety audit. > [!TIP] > **Why step-by-step?** The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"List the phases of safety life-cycle"* (3-mark) and *"Explain the role of SRS in PES development"* (7-mark). A structured bullet list fetches full marks. ## 2.2 The Three Subsystems of a SIF A SIF implemented by a PES consists of three independent subsystems in **series** (from a reliability viewpoint): $$\text{SIF} = \text{Sensor Subsystem} \;\times\; \text{Logic Solver} \;\times\; \text{Final Element Subsystem}$$ Each subsystem contributes to the total PFD: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}$$ (Additive rule applies because component PFDs are small and independent.) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 — Memorise with units!** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reliability Function (constant $\lambda$) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(0)=1,\; R(\infty)=0$ | | 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability | | 3 | Failure Rate ↔ MTTF | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | For non-repairable item | | 4 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Repairable item | | 5 | Series Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Multiplicative | | 6 | Parallel (active redundant) | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | **N** out of **N** | | 7 | PFD (low-demand, $\lambda t \ll 1$) | $\text{PFD} \approx \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T_i}{2}$ | $T_i$ = proof-test interval | | 8 | PFD for 1oo1 | $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$ | Single channel, dangerous failures only | | 9 | PFD for 1oo2 (voted) | $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_i)^2}{3}$ | Diagnostic coverage considered | | 10 | PFD for 2oo3 (each channel) | $\text{PFD}_{2oo3} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_i)^2}{3}$ | Best balance of safety & availability | | 11 | PFH (high-demand) | $\text{PFH} = \lambda_D$ | Or $\lambda_{DU} + 0.5\,\lambda_{DD}$ | | 12 | SIL Targets (low-demand) | $\text{SIL 1: } 10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$
$\text{SIL 2: } 10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$
$\text{SIL 3: } 10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | Per IEC 61511 | | 13 | SIL Targets (high-demand) | $\text{SIL 1: } 10^{-6} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$
$\text{SIL 2: } 10^{-7} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-6}$
$\text{SIL 3: } 10^{-8} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ | Continuous mode | | 14 | Spurious Trip Rate | $\text{STR} = \lambda_S \cdot 2$ (1oo2)
$\text{STR} = \lambda_S$ (2oo3) | Safe-failure side | | 15 | SFF | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic coverage proxy | > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do not confuse **dangerous-detected (DD)** and **dangerous-undetected (DU)** failures. The DU fraction is what determines SIL, because DD failures are caught by the PES's self-diagnostics and lead to a safe shutdown. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if you write $PFD \propto \lambda$ instead of $\text{PFD} \propto \lambda_{DU}$. ## 2.4 Logic Diagram — Anatomy of a PES A Logic Diagram in PES safety engineering represents the **functional flow** from process variable to mitigation, *not* the wiring. The canonical KTU representation is: ``` Process Variable (PV) ↓ [ Sensor 1 ] ← (signal conditioning, HART, smart transmitter) ↓ [ Signal Conditioning & Voting ] ↓ [ Logic Solver (PLC / DCS) ] ← (trip logic: high-high, low-low, rate) ↓ [ Output Card & Relay ] ↓ [ Final Element 1 & 2 ] ← (shutdown valve, breaker, vent) ↓ Safe State Achieved → Process brought to safe condition ↑ [ Proof Test / Diagnostics ] ← (online test, partial stroke test) ``` **Logic vs. Wiring Diagram:** A logic diagram uses **named variables** (e.g., `PT_101.HH` = Pressure Transmitter 101, High-High alarm), whereas a wiring diagram shows the physical loop. KTU asks to *differentiate* between them in 7-mark questions. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Petrochemical:** PES-based Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems on offshore platforms. - **Nuclear:** Reactor Protection System (RPS) – IEC 61513 derived. - **Process industry:** Burner Management Systems (BMS) for fired heaters. - **Automotive:** Drive-by-wire, brake-by-wire (ISO 26262 – functional safety cousin). - **Power:** Generator tripping, transformer differential protection. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Reliability Function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ **Assumption:** The hazard rate $\lambda$ is *constant* (memoryless / exponential distribution). This is the standard KTU assumption for electronic components in their useful life. Let: - $R(t)$ = probability that the system survives up to time $t$. - $F(t)$ = cumulative probability of failure by time $t$. - $f(t)$ = probability density of failure, $f(t) = \dfrac{dF}{dt}$. **Step 1 — Complement Rule.** By the laws of probability, the system is either *working* or *failed* at any instant $t$: $$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$ **Step 2 — Definition of Failure Rate.** The instantaneous failure rate (hazard) is defined as the *conditional* rate of failure, given survival up to $t$: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\,\frac{1}{R(t)}\,\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ (The negative sign arises because $f(t) = -dR/dt$ since $R$ is monotonically decreasing.) **Step 3 — Separation of Variables.** Rearrange to isolate $R(t)$: $$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt$$ **Step 4 — Integrate Both Sides.** For the constant-$\lambda$ case used in KTU problems, $\lambda(t) = \lambda$: $$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\,d\tau$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral.** $$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t$$ Since $R(0) = 1$ (the system is working at $t = 0$), $\ln R(0) = 0$. **Step 6 — Exponentiate.** $$\boxed{\,R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\,}$$ **Step 7 — Resulting MTTF.** The mean time to failure is the expected value of the failure time: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ This justifies the standard identity $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: PFD of a 1oo1 Architecture A 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) system is a **single non-redundant channel**. It demands the safety action, and the failure to act is a *dangerous undetected* failure. **Step 1 — Probability of Failure on Demand in $[0, T_i]$.** If a proof test is performed every $T_i$ hours, the average unavailability over a cycle is: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} F(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.** $$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} \bigl[1 - e^{-\lambda t}\bigr]\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate Term by Term.** $$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i}\left[T_i - \left(\frac{1 - e^{-\lambda T_i}}{\lambda}\right)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Series Expansion for Small $\lambda T_i$.** Since $\lambda T_i \ll 1$ in low-demand mode (typical: $\lambda \sim 10^{-6}/\text{h},\; T_i = 8760\,\text{h}$), expand $e^{-\lambda T_i}$: $$e^{-\lambda T_i} \approx 1 - \lambda T_i + \frac{(\lambda T_i)^2}{2}$$ So: $$1 - e^{-\lambda T_i} \approx \lambda T_i - \frac{(\lambda T_i)^2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Final Approximation.** $$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i}\left[T_i - \frac{\lambda T_i - \frac{(\lambda T_i)^2}{2}}{\lambda}\right] = \frac{\lambda T_i}{2} - \frac{(\lambda T_i)^2}{6}$$ **Step 6 — Dominant First-Order Term (KTU 2024 standard).** $$\boxed{\,\text{PFD}_{1oo1} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}\,T_i}{2}\,}$$ > [!TIP] > **Intuition:** The factor **½** arises because the *average* failure occurs at the *middle* of the proof-test interval. A system that fails at $t = 0$ is caught immediately, while a system that fails at $t = T_i$ is caught only at proof test — the average unavailability integrates to $\frac{1}{2}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: PFD of a 1oo2 Architecture (Voted) In a 1oo2 system, the safety action is taken if **either** of the two channels demands it. The system fails dangerously only if *both* channels fail dangerously in the same interval. **Step 1 — Series-of-Blocks View.** For two independent channels, the dangerous failure of channel 1 **AND** channel 2: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \text{PFD}_{\text{ch1}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{ch2}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\text{PFD}_{ch} = \lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$.** $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \left(\frac{\lambda_{DU}\,T_i}{2}\right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Refined Form (with $\beta$-factor for CCF, KTU advanced level).** Common-cause failure fraction $\beta$ adds: $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \beta\,\text{PFD}_{1oo1} + (1-\beta)\,\left(\frac{\lambda_{DU}\,T_i}{2}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Final Boxed Form (ignoring CCF for KTU basic).** $$\boxed{\,\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU}\,T_i)^2}{3}\,}$$ (The $1/3$ comes from averaging over a square distribution of joint-failure times, which is the IEC 61511 simplified form.) --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: SIL Determination from a PFD Calculation **Given:** A PES-based emergency cooling system has: - Sensor dangerous-undetected failure rate: $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{h}$ - Logic solver: $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{h}$ - Final element (solenoid + valve): $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{h}$ - Proof test interval: $T_i = 8760\,\text{h}$ (1 year) - Architecture: 1oo2 voted sensors, 1oo1 logic, 1oo2 voted valves **Step 1 — Sensor PFD (1oo2).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \frac{(5\times10^{-6} \times 8760)^2}{3} = \frac{(4.38\times10^{-2})^2}{3} \approx 6.4\times10^{-4}$$ **Step 2 — Logic Solver PFD (1oo1).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{logic}} = \frac{1\times10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 4.38\times10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Final Element PFD (1oo2).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{valve}} = \frac{(2\times10^{-6} \times 8760)^2}{3} \approx 1.02\times10^{-4}$$ **Step 4 — Sum (Series-Addition Rule).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 6.4\times10^{-4} + 4.38\times10^{-3} + 1.02\times10^{-4} = 5.10\times10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — Map to SIL.** $5.10 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $[10^{-3},\ 10^{-2})$ ⇒ **SIL 2**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner note:** KTU will accept the order of magnitude only if you **show the addition** (the rules are not strictly linear, but the simplified IEC 61511 formula is *additive* when PFDs are small). Skipping the addition step is a 2-mark loss. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Comparison (KTU Quick Reference) | Architecture | Voting | PFD Formula | Safe Failure Behaviour | Spurious Trip Rate | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **1oo1** | 1 of 1 | $\lambda_{DU} T_i/2$ | No redundancy | $\lambda_S$ | Low-consequence trips | | **1oo2** | 1 of 2 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2/3$ | High safety | $2\lambda_S$ | High-consequence, low-spurious-tolerance | | **2oo2** | 2 of 2 | $\lambda_{DU} T_i$ | High availability | $\lambda_S^2$ | Fire & gas, double-confirm | | **2oo3** | 2 of 3 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2/3$ | Best balance | $6\lambda_S^2$ | Reactor trips, turbine overspeed | | **1oo2D** | 1 of 2 with diag. | $\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2/3$ | Diagnostic redundancy | $2\lambda_S$ | Modern IEC 61508 SIL-3 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — PES Safety System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR procA[Process Variable] --> sensA[Sensor Subsystem] sensA --> sigA[Signal Conditioning] sigA --> votA[Voting Logic 1oo2] votA --> logA[Logic Solver PES PLC] logA --> outA[Output Module] outA --> finA[Final Element Solenoid] finA --> valA[Shutdown Valve] valA --> sfa[Safe State Achieved] logA -.feedback.-> diagA[Self Diagnostics] diagA -.Bypass.-> logA ptA[Proof Test Schedule] -.verifies.-> sensA ptA -.verifies.-> logA ptA -.verifies.-> finA ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Logic Diagram of a Typical PES-Based SIF (Over-Pressure Protection) ```mermaid flowchart TB in1[PT 101 Process Pressure Transmitter] --> cmp1[Comparator HH Setpoint] in2[PT 102 Redundant Pressure Transmitter] --> cmp2[Comparator HH Setpoint] cmp1 --> vote1[Voter OR 1oo2 Logic] cmp2 --> vote1 vote1 --> tripa[Trip Relay Coil] tripa --> sdv1[SDV 101 Shutdown Valve De energize to trip] sdv1 --> sstate1[Safe State: Vessel Isolated] vote1 --> hmi1[HMI Annunciation] vote1 --> dcs1[DCS Alarm Log] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) for 1oo1, 1oo2, and 2oo3 ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1oo1[1oo1 Architecture] a1[Input] --> b1[Single Channel] --> c1[Output] end subgraph S1oo2[1oo2 Architecture] a2[Input] --> b2[Channel A] a2 --> b3[Channel B] b2 --> c2[OR Voter] b3 --> c2 c2 --> d2[Output] end subgraph S2oo3[2oo3 Architecture] a3[Input] --> b4[Channel A] a3 --> b5[Channel B] a3 --> b6[Channel C] b4 --> c3[2oo3 Voter] b5 --> c3 b6 --> c3 c3 --> d3[Output] end ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Safety Life-Cycle (V-Model Simplified) ```mermaid flowchart TD c1[1 Concept] --> c2[2 Scope] c2 --> c3[3 Hazard Analysis] c3 --> c4[4 Safety Requirements SRS] c4 --> c5[5 Design Architecture] c5 --> c6[6 PES Implementation] c6 --> c7[7 Verification] c7 --> c8[8 Validation] c8 --> c9[9 Operation] c9 --> c10[10 Modification] c10 --> c11[11 Decommissioning] c7 -.verify.-> c5 c8 -.validate.-> c4 c5 -.realises.-> c4 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — PFD Calculation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD st1[Identify SIF Boundary] --> st2[List Components Sensor Logic Final] st2 --> st3[Obtain FMEDA Data lambda_DD lambda_DU lambda_S] st3 --> st4[Choose Architecture 1oo1 1oo2 2oo3] st4 --> st5[Apply PFD Formula per Channel] st5 --> st6[Sum PFDs of Subsystems] st6 --> st7[Compare with SIL Target Table] st7 --> st8{Within Target?} st8 -- Yes --> st9[SIL Achieved] st8 -- No --> st10[Increase TI Reduce Lambda Change Architecture] st10 --> st5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `procA`, `sensA`, `vote1`), no reserved keywords are used as standalone IDs, and all quoted labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — 3 Mark Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Define a Programmable Electronic System (PES) in the context of safety engineering. Mention two advantages over a hard-wired relay system.** **Model Answer (Verbatim Examiner Style):** A **Programmable Electronic System (PES)** is a computer-based control system employing microprocessors, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), or distributed control systems (DCS), used to perform safety functions in industrial processes. It is designed, validated, and operated to achieve a specified **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** as defined in IEC 61508/61511. **Advantages over relay systems:** 1. **Complex voting logic** (1oo2, 2oo3) can be implemented via software without re-wiring, enabling higher SIL with fewer physical channels. 2. **On-line self-diagnostics** detect dangerous-detected failures immediately, reducing the dangerous-undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$. *(Examiner: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two distinct advantages. Maximum 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between PFD and PFH. In which operating mode is each used?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Full Form | Mode of Operation | SIL Range | |---|---|---|---| | **PFD** | Probability of Failure on Demand | **Low-demand mode** (demand rate $\le 1$/year) | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ – $10^{-1}$ ; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-2}$ ; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-3}$ | | **PFH** | Probability of Failure per Hour | **High-demand or continuous mode** (demand rate $>$ 1/year) | SIL 1: $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-5}$ ; SIL 2: $10^{-7}$ – $10^{-6}$ ; SIL 3: $10^{-8}$ – $10^{-7}$ | **PFD** is dimensionless (a probability), while **PFH** has units of /hour (a frequency). The PES must be evaluated in the mode dictated by the SIF's operational profile. --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > KTU 2024 ESE Rule: Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** *With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the development of a PES safety system for an over-pressure protection application in a hydrocarbon reactor. Describe the role of each subsystem. (7 marks)* **(b)** *A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/h, a logic solver has $\lambda_{DU} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h, and a shutdown valve has $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h. The architecture is 1oo1 for sensor, 1oo1 for logic, and 1oo2 for valve. Proof test interval $T_i = 4380$ h (6 months). Calculate the total PFD and determine the SIL achieved. (7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Block Diagram (text-rendered for handwritten answer):** ``` Process (Reactor) │ ▼ Pressure Transmitter (PT) ──► Trip Amplifier / PLC (Logic Solver) │ ▼ Solenoid → SDV (Shutdown Valve) │ ▼ Safe State: Vent opens / Feed stops ``` **Development Steps:** 1. **Hazard Identification:** Over-pressure can rupture the reactor → HAZOP node "High Pressure" on the reactor. 2. **Risk Graph / LOPA:** Determine required risk reduction factor (RRF) → target SIL. 3. **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS):** "On PT reading $\ge P_{HH}$, the SDV must close within 2 s, with SIL 2 integrity." 4. **Subsystem Roles:** - **Sensor (PT):** Converts process pressure to a 4–20 mA / HART signal; self-diagnostic enabled. - **Logic Solver (PES PLC):** Compares the signal to the high-high setpoint; performs debounce; energises the trip relay. - **Final Element (Solenoid + SDV):** De-energises to trip (fail-safe); vents the reactor. 5. **Proof Testing:** Manual or partial stroke test every $T_i$. 6. **Validation:** FMEDA, SIL verification, documentation per IEC 61511. *(Examiner: 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks for development steps, 3 marks for subsystem roles.)* --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 — PFD of Sensor (1oo1).** $$\text{PFD}_{S} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}\,T_i}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380}{2} = 6.57 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2 — PFD of Logic Solver (1oo1).** $$\text{PFD}_{L} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380}{2} = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — PFD of Valve (1oo2).** $$\text{PFD}_{V} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU}\,T_i)^{2}}{3} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380)^{2}}{3} = \frac{(6.57 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{3} = 1.44 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4 — Total PFD (Series-Addition Rule).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 6.57 \times 10^{-3} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3} + 1.44 \times 10^{-5} \approx 7.66 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — SIL Determination.** $7.66 \times 10^{-3} \in [10^{-3},\ 10^{-2})$ ⇒ **SIL 2 achieved.** *(Examiner: 2 marks for sensor PFD, 2 marks for valve PFD, 1 mark for sum, 2 marks for correct SIL conclusion.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative] **(a)** *Explain the safety life-cycle of a PES with a neat sketch. State the importance of the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS) phase. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Compare 1oo2 and 2oo3 voting architectures in terms of PFD, spurious trip rate, availability, and typical industrial applications. (7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — (a) The **Safety Life-Cycle** is an IEC 61508 framework that defines all phases from concept to decommissioning. **Phases (Examiner: 1 mark for listing):** 1. Concept 2. Scope definition 3. Hazard & risk analysis 4. Safety requirements specification 5. Design & engineering 6. Implementation 7. Verification 8. Validation 9. Operation & maintenance 10. Modification 11. Decommissioning **V-Model sketch (Examiner: 2 marks):** The life-cycle is shaped like a 'V' — design stages descend on the left; verification/validation stages ascend on the right. Each design phase is matched to a corresponding test phase: - Design ↔ Verification - Implementation ↔ Validation - SRS ↔ FAT/SAT **Importance of SRS (Examiner: 4 marks):** The **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** is the *contract* between process safety, instrumentation, and control disciplines. It specifies: - **Functional requirements** — what the SIF must do (e.g., close SDV on PT-HH). - **Integrity requirements** — required SIL, PFD/PFH target. - **Operational requirements** — proof test interval, response time, environmental conditions. A poor SRS is the **root cause of >50% of PES-related industrial incidents** (per CCPS). The KTU examiner expects the student to mention at least these three classes of requirements. --- #### Model Solution — (b) | Parameter | **1oo2 Architecture** | **2oo3 Architecture** | |---|---|---| | **PFD** | $(\lambda_{DU}T_i)^2 / 3$ | $(\lambda_{DU}T_i)^2 / 3$ (similar order) | | **Spurious Trip Rate (STR)** | $2 \lambda_S$ (high) | $6 \lambda_S^{2}$ (very low) | | **Availability** | Lower (more spurious) | Higher (one channel can fail safely) | | **Fault Tolerance** | Tolerates 1 dangerous failure | Tolerates 1 dangerous failure (with one more tolerable safe failure) | | **SIL Achieved** | Up to SIL 3 | Up to SIL 3 | | **Cost** | Medium | High (3 channels) | | **Industrial Use** | Burner management, ESD on offshore | Reactor trips, turbine overspeed, fire & gas | **Conclusion:** For applications demanding **high safety** and **moderate availability**, **1oo2** is preferred. For applications where **spurious trips are very costly** (lost production, safety hazard themselves), **2oo3** is preferred. *(Examiner: 2 marks for PFD comparison, 2 marks for STR, 1 mark availability, 2 marks application distinction.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $\lambda$ with $\lambda_{DU}$:** Always show the dangerous-undetected fraction. Writing PFD = $\lambda T_i / 2$ without the DU subscript will lose 1 mark. > 2. **Series rule vs. multiplication:** The PFDs of subsystems (sensor, logic, valve) are *added*, not *multiplied*. Multiplication is for *parallel/identical* channels. This is the single most common mistake in KTU answer sheets. > 3. **Wrong SIL range:** Always cite the IEC 61511 table ranges, not IEC 61508. KTU follows 61511 for process industries. > 4. **Spelling "Fail Safe" as "Failsafe":** Both are accepted, but consistency is rewarded. > 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** $\lambda$ must be in /h, $T_i$ in h, PFD dimensionless — lose ½ mark if units missing. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **PES = Programmable Electronic System**, the modern computer-based realisation of a safety control system, replacing hard-wired relays. - [x] **IEC 61508** = generic functional safety standard; **IEC 61511** = process-industry sector standard. - [x] **SIF** = Safety Instrumented Function; **SIS** = Safety Instrumented System (collection of SIFs). - [x] **Reliability function** under constant failure rate: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. - [x] **PFD₁₀₀₁ ≈ λ_{DU} T_i / 2** — single-channel approximation. - [x] **PFD₁₀₀₂ ≈ (λ_{DU} T_i)² / 3** — voted 1-of-2. - [x] **PFD₂₀₀₃ ≈ (λ_{DU} T_i)² / 3** — voted 2-of-3, low spurious trip. - [x] **PFD_SIF = PFD_sensor + PFD_logic + PFD_valve** (series addition). - [x] **SIL ranges (low-demand):** SIL 1: $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$, SIL 2: $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$, SIL 3: $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$. - [x] **SIL ranges (high-demand, PFH):** SIL 1: $10^{-6}$–$10^{-5}$/h, SIL 2: $10^{-7}$–$10^{-6}$/h, SIL 3: $10^{-8}$–$10^{-7}$/h. - [x] **Proof test interval $T_i$** is the *lever* — halving $T_i$ quarters the PFD (order-wise for 1oo1, squares for 1oo2). - [x] **Common-cause failures (CCF)** quantified via $\beta$-factor; ignored in basic KTU problems but expected at M.Tech level. - [x] **Logic diagram ≠ Wiring diagram** — logic uses named variables (PT_101.HH), wiring uses physical loops. - [x] **V-Model life-cycle** has 11 phases; SRS is the most critical. - [x] **Three subsystems of a SIF**: Sensor, Logic Solver, Final Element — all in series from a PFD perspective. - [x] **Diagnostic coverage** reduces $\lambda_{DU}$ to $\lambda_{DU} (1 - DC)$ — handle this in advanced problems.

topics.title: Calculation of Fractional Dead Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Fractional Dead Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fractional Dead Time (f)** is a fundamental reliability and safety-instrumentation parameter defined as the *average fraction of time that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) or protective system is unable to perform its intended safety action upon demand*. In mathematical terms, it represents the long-run probability that a dangerous (unrevealed) failure is present in the protective channel at the instant a hazardous demand occurs.

For low-demand mode operation (per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**), the Fractional Dead Time is numerically equal to the **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg)**:

$$f \;=\; \text{PFD}_{avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt$$

where $F(t)$ is the instantaneous unavailability function of the protective channel and $T$ is the **Proof Test Interval (PTI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PESFT751 – Module 1):** Fractional Dead Time is the foundation for computing the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of a SIF, and it directly determines whether a protective system meets its target **PFD_avg** budget for a given SIL (1, 2, or 3).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **smoke detector in your home**. Most of the time, it sits silently — it is *armed but never tested for actual response*. Between factory inspections (proof tests), a tiny electronic component might silently degrade, so that on the *one day* a real fire breaks out, the detector *might* fail to sound the alarm.

- The **time the detector is silently broken** is its "dead time."
- The **fraction** of the year that the detector is silently broken is the **Fractional Dead Time**.
- If you proof-test it every 12 months and the dangerous failure rate is very small, the detector is "dead" for only a tiny fraction of the year.

A Fractional Dead Time of **0.01** means the protective system is unavailable for about **1% of its operational life** — which corresponds to **SIL 2** under IEC 61511.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Dangerous Failure Rate (λ_D)** — expressed in **failures per hour (fph)** or **per year**.
- **Proof Test Interval (T)** — typically expressed in **hours** or **years**.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** — fraction of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostics.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** — proportion of failures that do not lead to a hazardous event.

> [!NOTE]
> A dangerous failure is *unrevealed* if the system continues to look "healthy" (no spurious trip). These unrevealed failures accumulate silently between proof tests and are the direct cause of Fractional Dead Time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Fractional Dead Time

The protective channel alternates between two conditions:

1. **Healthy (Available) State** — channel can respond to demand.
2. **Silently Failed (Dangerous-Undetected) State** — channel cannot respond, but the failure is invisible until the next proof test.

The instantaneous unavailability $F(t)$ since the last proof test follows an exponential decay law:

$$F(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \cdot t} \;\approx\; \lambda_D \cdot t \quad (\text{for } \lambda_D t \ll 1)$$

The *Why*: The proof test restores the channel to the "healthy" state at $t = 0$. As time elapses, the probability of a dangerous failure grows linearly (small-failure approximation).

The *How*: We integrate this instantaneous unavailability across one complete proof test interval $T$ and divide by $T$ to obtain the *time-averaged* unavailability — that is, the **Fractional Dead Time**.

### 2.2 General Single-Channel Expression

For an **unrevealed** dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and a proof test interval $T$ (constant, perfect, complete restoration):

$$f_{1oo1} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$

### 2.3 Architecture-Dependent Formulas (IEC 61508 Simplified)

| Architecture | Voting Logic | Mathematical Derivation | **Fractional Dead Time (f = PFD_avg)** | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **1oo1** | Single channel must trip | $F(t) = \lambda_D t$ | $f = \dfrac{\lambda_D \, T}{2}$ | Simple low-criticality loops |
| **1oo2** | Both channels must trip | $F(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2$ | $f = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \, T)^2}{3}$ | High integrity, redundancy for safety |
| **2oo2** | Both channels must trip | $F(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2$ | $f = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \, T)^2}{3}$ | Avoid nuisance trips |
| **2oo3** | 2 of 3 channels must trip | $F(t) = 6(\lambda_D t)^2$ | $f = \dfrac{6(\lambda_D \, T)^2}{3} = 2(\lambda_D T)^2$ | High availability + high integrity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy Impact:** Moving from 1oo1 to 1oo2 reduces $f$ by *orders of magnitude* because the failure probability is *squared*. However, the channel failure rate must remain in the small-failure regime (${\lambda_D T \le 0.1}$) for the simplified formulas to hold.

### 2.4 Inclusion of Diagnostic Coverage (β-Model Simplified)

If automatic diagnostics detect a fraction **DC** of dangerous failures, the *effective* dangerous undetected failure rate becomes:

$$\lambda_{DU} \;=\; \lambda_D \cdot (1 - DC)$$

The Fractional Dead Time is then computed using $\lambda_{DU}$ in place of $\lambda_D$:

$$f \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T}{2} \;\;(\text{1oo1})$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SIL Verification**: SIL targets (PFD_avg between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-5}$) are quantified using $f$.
- **Proof Test Interval Optimisation**: Longer intervals reduce maintenance cost but increase $f$.
- **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**: $f$ feeds into the independent protection layer (IPL) credit.
- **Insurance / Regulatory Compliance**: TÜV, OSHA, and offshore regulators (UK HSE) mandate quantitative proof of $f$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation for 1oo1 Architecture

**Step 1 — Model the channel.** The protective channel has a constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_D$. We assume a perfect proof test at $t = 0$ that restores full availability, i.e.:

$$A(0) \;=\; 1 \quad \text{(fully available at the start of the test interval)}$$

**Step 2 — Write the instantaneous availability.** With an exponential time-to-failure distribution (memoryless property):

$$A(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_D \, t}$$

**Step 3 — Write the instantaneous unavailability.** Since $F(t) + A(t) = 1$:

$$F(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \, t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-failure approximation.** Because $\lambda_D T \ll 1$ for any realistic protective system (e.g., $\lambda_D \approx 10^{-6}$ /hr, $T \approx 8760$ hr → $\lambda_D T \approx 0.0088$):

$$F(t) \;\approx\; \lambda_D \, t$$

**Step 5 — Average over the proof test interval.** By definition of fractional dead time:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{1oo1} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \lambda_D \, t\,dt \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D}{T}\left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{T} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D \, T}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,f_{1oo1} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D \, T}{2}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation for 1oo2 Architecture

**Step 1 — Failure criterion.** A 1oo2 system fails dangerously only when **both** channels fail dangerously (AND logic for safety). The instantaneous unavailability is the *product* of the two single-channel unavailabilities:

$$F(t) \;=\; (1 - e^{-\lambda_D \, t})^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-failure approximation.** For $\lambda_D t \ll 1$:

$$F(t) \;\approx\; (\lambda_D \, t)^2$$

**Step 3 — Average over the proof test interval.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{1oo2} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_D \, t)^2 \, dt \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T} t^2 \, dt \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T}\left[\frac{t^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{T} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D^{\,2} \, T^{2}}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,f_{1oo2} \;=\; \frac{(\lambda_D \, T)^{2}}{3}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation for 2oo3 Architecture (Majority Voting)

**Step 1 — Failure criterion.** The 2oo3 SIF fails dangerously when **any two of the three** channels fail dangerously. There are $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ such combinations.

**Step 2 — Instantaneous unavailability.** With $\lambda_D t \ll 1$, the binomial expansion of $(1 - e^{-\lambda_D t})^3$ truncated at the second-order term:

$$F(t) \;\approx\; 3\,(\lambda_D t)^{2} \cdot 1 \;+\; (\lambda_D t)^{3} \;\approx\; 3(\lambda_D t)^2$$

(Note: some IEC references quote the leading coefficient as $3$ and others as $6$ depending on inclusion of the third-order residual; the conservative bound used here follows the simplified formula $f = 6(\lambda_D T)^2 / 3$.)

**Step 3 — Time-averaged value.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{2oo3} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 6(\lambda_D t)^2\,dt \;=\; \frac{6\,\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T}\cdot\frac{T^{3}}{3} \;=\; 2(\lambda_D T)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,f_{2oo3} \;\approx\; 2\,(\lambda_D T)^2\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Comparison

Assume $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr and $T = 8760$ hr (1 year). Then $\lambda_D T = 0.0438$.

| Architecture | PFD_avg (f) | Order of Magnitude | Equivalent SIL Band |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1oo1** | $1.095 \times 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-2}$ | SIL 1 (border) |
| **1oo2** | $6.40 \times 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-4}$ | SIL 2 |
| **2oo3** | $3.84 \times 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 (high end) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **2oo3** architecture offers higher *availability* (fault tolerance) but does **not** automatically yield the lowest $f$ — the 1oo2 is the safest in terms of average unavailability because *both* channels must fail to cause a hazard.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Drivers of Fractional Dead Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazardous Plant Demand"] --> B["SIF Initiates Protective Action"]
    B --> C{"SIF Available?"}
    C -- "YES" --> D["Safe Shutdown Achieved"]
    C -- "NO" --> E["Hazard Realised - Incident"]
    F["Dangerous Undetected Failure Rate lambda_DU"] --> G["Proof Test Interval T"]
    G --> H["Fractional Dead Time f"]
    F --> H
    H --> I["Architecture 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3"]
    I --> J["SIL Verification PFD_avg"]
    K["Diagnostic Coverage DC"] --> F
    L["Common Cause Failure Beta"] --> F
    J --> M["Compliance with IEC 61508 61511"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Proof Test Cycle & FDT Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Proof Test Completed - t equals 0"] --> nodeB["Channel Healthy - A equals 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Time Elapses - t increases"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Unavailability F of t equals lambda_D times t"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Time Average over interval T"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Fractional Dead Time f equals lambda_D T over 2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Next Proof Test Scheduled - Cycle Repeats"]
```

### 4.3 MooN Voting Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input["Process Variable Input"]
        I1["Channel A Sensor"]
        I2["Channel B Sensor"]
        I3["Channel C Sensor"]
    end
    I1 --> V["Voting Logic 2oo3 - 2 of 3 must agree"]
    I2 --> V
    I3 --> V
    V --> O["Final Trip Command to Final Element"]
    O --> SIF["Safety Instrumented Function SIL Target"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block-diagram above depicts the **functional safety flow**. In a physical layout, sensors feed a logic solver (e.g., a Safety PLC) that implements the **M-out-of-N** voting rule. The output drives a final element (valve, breaker) to bring the plant to a safe state.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Fractional Dead Time" as applied to a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). State its relationship with the Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fractional Dead Time (f) is the **long-run average fraction of operating time** during which a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is *unable* to perform its intended safety action due to an unrevealed dangerous failure.

For a SIF operating in **low-demand mode**, the Fractional Dead Time is numerically equal to the **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg)**, i.e.,

$$f \;=\; \text{PFD}_{avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt$$

It is a dimensionless quantity (often expressed on a logarithmic scale) that quantifies the operational readiness of the protective layer. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for relationship, 1 for the integral expression.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*A pressure transmitter has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T = 8760$ hours (one year). Calculate the Fractional Dead Time for a 1oo1 architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{1oo1} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D \, T}{2} \;=\; \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.01752}{2} \;=\; 8.76 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $f_{1oo1} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$, corresponding to **SIL 1** compliance (PFD range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$). *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for result and SIL identification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q5A. (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Derive the expression for the Fractional Dead Time of a 1oo1 protective channel operating in low-demand mode with a constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and proof test interval $T$. State all assumptions clearly.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Failure rate is **constant** (exponential distribution of time to failure).
2. Proof tests are **perfect** (i.e., the channel is restored to "as-good-as-new" condition after each test).
3. The dangerous failures are **undetected** by automatic diagnostics during operation.
4. Low-demand operation (demands much less frequent than proof tests).
5. Small-failure approximation holds: $\lambda_D T \ll 1$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The instantaneous availability at time $t$ after a successful proof test is:

$$A(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_D \, t}$$

The instantaneous unavailability is:

$$F(t) \;=\; 1 - A(t) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \, t}$$

Using the small-failure approximation $\lambda_D t \ll 1$, expand the exponential:

$$F(t) \;\approx\; \lambda_D \, t$$

By definition, the Fractional Dead Time is the time-averaged unavailability over the proof test interval:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{1oo1} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F(t)\,dt \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \lambda_D \, t \, dt \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D}{T}\left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{T} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_D \, T}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{f_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_D T}{2}}$ *(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 5 for derivation.)*

---

**Q5A. (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*A Safety Instrumented Function uses a 1oo2 architecture. Each channel has a dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T = 17520$ hours (two years).*
*(i) Calculate the Fractional Dead Time of the SIF.*
*(ii) Identify the SIL band in which the SIF operates (use IEC 61511 SIL table).*
*(iii) If the user shortens the proof test interval to $T = 4380$ hours (six months), what is the new Fractional Dead Time? Comment on the trade-off.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) FDT for 1oo2 (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{1oo2} \;=\; \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \, T)^{2}}{3} \;=\; \frac{[(1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17520]^{2}}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the inner term:

$$\lambda_{DU} T \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520 \;=\; 0.02628$$

Squaring and dividing:

$$f_{1oo2} \;=\; \frac{(0.02628)^{2}}{3} \;=\; \frac{6.906 \times 10^{-4}}{3} \;=\; 2.302 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) SIL Identification (2 marks):**

A PFD_avg of $2.3 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$, which corresponds to **SIL 2** under IEC 61511.

**(iii) New FDT after reducing T to 4380 hr (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU} T_{new} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 \;=\; 6.57 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{1oo2,\,new} \;=\; \frac{(6.57 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{3} \;=\; \frac{4.316 \times 10^{-5}}{3} \;=\; 1.439 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Trade-off Comment (1 mark):** Reducing $T$ from 17520 hr to 4380 hr improves FDT by a factor of $\approx 16$, but it requires **four times** as many proof tests per unit time, increasing **maintenance cost**, **plant downtime**, and **man-hour exposure**. The optimum balances safety integrity with operational expenditure.

---

**Q5B. (a) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Compare the Fractional Dead Time of a 1oo2 and a 2oo3 architecture for identical sensor failure rates $\lambda_D$ and proof test interval $T$. Under what practical situation would you recommend 2oo3 over 1oo2?* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Comparison (4 marks):**

For identical $\lambda_D$ and $T$:

$$f_{1oo2} \;=\; \frac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$$

$$f_{2oo3} \;\approx\; 2(\lambda_D T)^2 \;=\; \frac{6(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$$

The ratio is:

$$\frac{f_{2oo3}}{f_{1oo2}} \;\approx\; \frac{6}{1} \;=\; 6$$

So, the 1oo2 architecture yields a Fractional Dead Time that is **six times lower** than 2oo3 for the same channel failure rate. The 1oo2 architecture is therefore *safer* (lower PFD_avg) because both channels must fail dangerously for a hazardous outcome.

**When to prefer 2oo3 (3 marks):**

The 2oo3 architecture is preferred when:

1. **High availability** is also critical (production continuity) — the system tolerates a single-channel failure without tripping, unlike 1oo2.
2. **Spurious trip cost is very high** — false shutdowns cause major economic loss (e.g., LNG trains, petrochemical crackers).
3. **Diagnostics are unreliable** — voting across three channels can mask a single faulty channel while preserving safety.

In practice, 2oo3 is used in **high-consequence, high-production-loss** processes like offshore emergency shutdown (ESD) systems, turbine overspeed protection, and boiler flame-failure interlocks.

---

**Q5B. (b) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*A SIF has the following data:*
- *Channel dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour.*
- *Diagnostic coverage: $DC = 60\%$.*
- *Proof test interval: $T = 8760$ hours.*
- *Architecture: 1oo1.*

*(i) Compute the effective $\lambda_{DU}$.*
*(ii) Calculate the Fractional Dead Time including diagnostics.*
*(iii) How does increasing $DC$ to 90% affect the FDT?* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Effective λ_DU (2 marks):**

The portion of dangerous failures that are *undetected* by diagnostics is $(1 - DC)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU} \;=\; \lambda_D \cdot (1 - DC) \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6} \times (1 - 0.60) \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.40 \;=\; 1.6 \times 10^{-6} \;/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) FDT with DC = 60% (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{DU} \, T}{2} \;=\; \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.014016}{2} \;=\; 7.008 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) FDT with DC = 90% (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{DU}' \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.10 \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-7} \;/\text{hr}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f' \;=\; \frac{(4 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760}{2} \;=\; \frac{3.504 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \;=\; 1.752 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference:** Increasing DC from 60% → 90% reduces FDT by a factor of 4 (from $7.01 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1.75 \times 10^{-3}$), shifting the SIF closer to the SIL 2 boundary. **Diagnostic coverage is therefore a powerful and cost-effective SIL-enhancement lever**, often cheaper than increasing redundancy or shortening proof-test intervals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2:** Students frequently write $f = \lambda_D T$ instead of $\lambda_D T / 2$ for 1oo1 — this is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 2. **Using $\lambda_D$ instead of $\lambda_{DU}$:** When diagnostic coverage is given, examiners expect $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D (1 - DC)$. Mixing these up costs the entire sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting to square for redundancy:** In 1oo2, students often write $f = \lambda_D T / 2$ again instead of $(\lambda_D T)^2 / 3$. This is a **critical conceptual error**.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** A derivation without listed assumptions (constant $\lambda_D$, perfect proof tests, low-demand, small-failure) loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong SIL mapping:** Memorise the bands — SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$, SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FDT = Average unavailability = PFD_avg** (low-demand mode).
- **1oo1:** $f = \lambda_D T / 2$ — simplest single-channel loop.
- **1oo2 / 2oo2:** $f = (\lambda_D T)^2 / 3$ — redundancy squared reduces $f$ dramatically.
- **2oo3:** $f \approx 2(\lambda_D T)^2$ — high availability, moderate FDT.
- **Diagnostic coverage:** $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D (1 - DC)$ always replaces $\lambda_D$ when diagnostics exist.
- **Proof-test interval ($T$):** Halving $T$ halves $f$ for 1oo1, but quarters $f$ for 1oo2 (squared dependence).
- **Assumptions checklist:** constant $\lambda$, perfect proof test, low-demand, small-failure, independent failures (ignore common-cause for first-order calculation).
- **SIL bands (IEC 61511):** SIL 1 [$10^{-2}$, $10^{-1}$), SIL 2 [$10^{-3}$, $10^{-2}$), SIL 3 [$10^{-4}$, $10^{-3}$), SIL 4 [$10^{-5}$, $10^{-4}$).
- **Trade-off levers:** shorter $T$ → safer but costlier; redundancy → safer but more hardware; higher DC → safer with minimal hardware change.
- **Always state units:** $\lambda$ in /hr or /yr; $T$ must use the same time unit as $\lambda$.
- **Memorise the integration limits** $0$ to $T$ — derivation marks are awarded explicitly for the integral setup.
- **Numerical sanity check:** For realistic SIFs, $\lambda_D T$ should be **≤ 0.1**; otherwise the simplified formulas are invalid and Markov modelling is required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT751 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Safety Engineering and Management**, the term **"Application"** within Module 1 refers to the systematic deployment of safety engineering principles, hazard identification techniques, risk assessment methodologies, and control frameworks across diverse industrial and operational environments. It encompasses the practical translation of theoretical safety knowledge into tangible engineering controls, administrative procedures, and management systems that prevent workplace injuries, occupational illnesses, and catastrophic incidents.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes for PESFT751, *application* in safety engineering signifies the competency to **identify**, **evaluate**, **control**, and **monitor** workplace hazards using established engineering, regulatory, and managerial tools such as HAZOP, FMEA, Job Safety Analysis (JSA), Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), and the Hierarchy of Controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Application" domain in Module 1 tests the student's ability to map theoretical safety models (from earlier sub-modules like Hazard, Risk, Accident Theories) onto **real industrial scenarios** such as construction sites, chemical process plants, manufacturing units, mining operations, and oil & gas installations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine safety engineering application as the process of **building a fire-resistant house before the fire arrives**.

- **Theory (the blueprint):** Knowing that fire exists, understanding how it spreads, learning about fire-resistant materials.
- **Application (the actual construction):** Installing smoke detectors, using fire-retardant paint, creating evacuation routes, placing fire extinguishers — every physical, actionable step taken **before** disaster strikes.

In the same way, *application* in safety engineering is the **"construction phase"** — it is where the engineer stops merely *knowing* about hazards and begins *doing* something tangible about them.

## 1.3 Fundamental Applications of Safety Engineering

The applications can be broadly classified into the following five pillars, each of which has been adopted as global best practice and is examinable under KTU Module 1:

| # | Application Domain | Core Focus |
|---|--------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA)** | Detecting and quantifying workplace dangers |
| 2 | **Process Safety Management (PSM)** | Preventing catastrophic chemical/process incidents |
| 3 | **Occupational Health & Safety (OHS)** | Protecting workers from long-term and acute harm |
| 4 | **Emergency Preparedness & Response (EPR)** | Minimizing damage during incidents |
| 5 | **Safety Management Systems (SMS)** | Continuous improvement via PDCA cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The universally accepted safety performance metric is the **Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR)**, defined as:
> $$\text{TRIR} = \frac{\text{Number of Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Hours Worked}}$$
> where **200,000** is the standard base representing **100 full-time employees working 40 hours/week for 50 weeks/year**.

---

## 1.4 Application in the Context of KTU 2024 Module 1

Module 1 of PESFT751 introduces the foundational lexicon of safety engineering. The "Application" topic serves as the **bridge** between conceptual learning (definitions, theories, classifications) and **practical industrial implementation**. The KTU examiner typically frames application questions around:

- A given industrial scenario (e.g., ammonia storage tank, crane operation, confined space entry)
- A requirement to select the appropriate hazard identification tool
- A demand to apply the Hierarchy of Controls
- A need to compute risk scores using a matrix

This topic directly addresses the following KTU Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental concepts of safety engineering and management.*
- **CO2:** *Apply hazard identification and risk assessment techniques to industrial scenarios.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Application Framework

The application of safety engineering in any industrial setting follows a structured, repeatable five-step framework. Each step is examinable under KTU Module 1.

### Step 1: Hazard Identification (HAZID)

The first application step is **finding** the hazards before they find you. Several industry-standard techniques are used:

- **Checklist Analysis** — A simple, pre-prepared list of known hazards.
- **What-If Analysis** — Brainstorming "What if this fails?" scenarios.
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — A structured team-based review using guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN).
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Systematic evaluation of component-level failures.

### Step 2: Risk Estimation

Once hazards are identified, their **risk** must be quantified. Risk is universally defined as:

$$R = f(S, P)$$

where:
- $R$ = Risk (magnitude of expected loss)
- $S$ = Severity (consequence of the hazard)
- $P$ = Probability (likelihood of occurrence)

### Step 3: Risk Evaluation (Risk Matrix Method)

The most KTU-frequently examined application method is the **Risk Matrix (L × S)**:

| Severity \ Likelihood | L1 (Rare) | L2 (Unlikely) | L3 (Possible) | L4 (Likely) | L5 (Almost Certain) |
|------------------------|-----------|---------------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **S5 (Catastrophic)** | Medium | High | Extreme | Extreme | Extreme |
| **S4 (Major)** | Medium | Medium | High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **S3 (Moderate)** | Low | Medium | High | High | Extreme |
| **S2 (Minor)** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **S1 (Negligible)** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

### Step 4: Risk Control (Hierarchy of Controls)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is the cornerstone application principle. It ranks control measures from most effective to least effective:

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard (most effective).
2. **Substitution** — Replace the hazard with something less dangerous.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate people from the hazard (guards, ventilation, interlocks).
4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures (training, signage, rotation).
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Protect the worker (last resort).

### Step 5: Monitoring & Review

Applications are never "one and done." Continuous monitoring via the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) ensures ongoing effectiveness.

## 2.2 Industrial Applications of Safety Engineering

### 2.2.1 Application in the Chemical Process Industry

In chemical plants, the primary application is **Process Safety Management (PSM)**. Engineers apply:

- **HAZOP** for design-stage hazard review.
- **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** to evaluate independent protection layers.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** using software like DNV PHAST or SAFETI.

The threshold for categorizing a chemical facility as high-risk is the **OSHA PSM Standard 29 CFR 1910.119**, which applies to processes involving more than **10,000 lbs (4,535 kg)** of a listed highly hazardous chemical.

### 2.2.2 Application in the Construction Industry

The **OSHA Construction Standard 29 CFR 1926** and Indian **BOCW Act, 1996** govern safety application. Key application areas include:

- Fall protection (mandatory above **6 feet / 1.8 m**).
- Scaffolding safety.
- Excavation safety (cave-in protection at depths > **1.2 m**).
- Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) compliance.

### 2.2.3 Application in the Manufacturing Industry

Lean Six Sigma methodologies intersect with safety applications through the **EHS (Environment, Health & Safety)** framework. The application includes:

- Machine guarding (compliance with **ANSI B11.19**).
- Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures.
- Industrial hygiene monitoring (noise < **85 dBA** over 8 hours, per OSHA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constant to Remember:** The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for occupational noise exposure is **90 dBA** for 8 hours (using a 5 dB exchange rate) or **85 dBA** for 8 hours (using a 3 dB exchange rate under NIOSH recommendations).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Application Context |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Risk Score** | $R = L \times S$ | Dimensionless | Risk Matrix evaluation |
| 2 | **TRIR** | $\text{TRIR} = \frac{\text{Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Hours Worked}}$ | per 100 FTE | Safety performance KPI |
| 3 | **Fatality Frequency Rate (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Hours Worked}}$ | per $10^8$ hours | High-hazard industries |
| 4 | **Incidence Rate** | $\text{IR} = \frac{\text{New Cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Hours Worked}}$ | per 100 FTE | OSHA-style reporting |
| 5 | **Severity Rate** | $\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Lost Days} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Hours Worked}}$ | days per 100 FTE | Lost-time injury analysis |
| 6 | **Noise Dose** | $D = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \ldots + \frac{C_n}{T_n}$ | % | Industrial hygiene |
| 7 | **F-N Curve (Fatality Risk)** | $P(f) = \frac{A}{N^n}$ | Probability | QRA, land-use planning |
| 8 | **Acceptable Risk (Individual)** | $\text{IRPA} \leq 10^{-5}$ /year | Probability | Public exposure limit |
| 9 | **ALARP** | $\text{Risk} \leq \text{As Low As Reasonably Practicable}$ | Qualitative | Cost-benefit decision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic curiosities. The **F-N Curve** is used by planning authorities worldwide to determine whether a hazardous installation (e.g., an LNG terminal near a residential zone) poses tolerable risk to the surrounding population. The pair $(A, n)$ for the original Rasmussen criteria was $(10^{-1}, 1)$ for the **lower bound** and $(10^{-3}, 2)$ for the **upper bound** of acceptable societal risk.

## 2.4 Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why HAZID first?** Because you cannot control what you have not identified. Approximately **76%** of industrial accidents investigated by the U.S. Chemical Safety Board (CSB) had causes that could have been identified through HAZOP if it had been properly conducted.
- **Why the Hierarchy of Controls?** Because PPE-dependent programs fail when workers do not wear PPE. Engineering controls are passive and reliable.
- **Why PDCA?** Because safety is a dynamic process — new equipment, new chemicals, new workers mean that yesterday's controls may not work tomorrow.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The application of safety engineering saves lives at scale. Consider the following benchmark data:

- After DuPont implemented its **"Safety and Health Vision"** in 1985, its injury rate dropped from over **9.0 recordable injuries per 100 employees** to under **0.5** by 2005.
- The U.K. HSE's **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)"** principle has reduced offshore oil & gas fatalities in the North Sea from **40+ deaths per year** in the 1970s to a near-zero level by the 2010s.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: TRIR from First Principles

The **Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR)** is one of the most commonly tested formulas in KTU Module 1. Here is its complete derivation:

### Step 1: Define the Base Working Hours

A standard full-time worker works:
- **40 hours/week**
- **50 weeks/year**
- Therefore, **1 FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) = 40 × 50 = 2,000 hours/year**

### Step 2: Standardize the Base

Since accident statistics are usually reported per **100 full-time workers** for comparability across companies of different sizes:
- **Base hours = 100 FTE × 2,000 hours/year = 200,000 hours/year**

### Step 3: Define the Rate

Let:
- $N$ = Total number of recordable injuries in the period
- $H$ = Total hours worked by all employees in the period

The injury rate per hour is:

$$\text{Rate per hour} = \frac{N}{H}$$

### Step 4: Scale to the Standard Base

Multiplying by 200,000 hours (the standard base):

$$\text{TRIR} = \frac{N}{H} \times 200{,}000$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\text{TRIR} = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A TRIR of 3.0 means that for every 100 full-time employees, **3 recordable injuries** occur in one year. The lower the TRIR, the safer the workplace.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Risk Score Computation Using L × S Matrix

### Step 1: Identify Likelihood (L)

Assign a numerical value to the likelihood of occurrence based on historical data or expert judgment:

$$L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

where:
- $L = 1$: Occurs in rare, exceptional circumstances (< 1 in 100 years)
- $L = 2$: Unlikely to occur in the project lifetime (1 in 10–100 years)
- $L = 3$: Possible (1 in 1–10 years)
- $L = 4$: Likely (occurs multiple times per year)
- $L = 5$: Almost certain (occurs frequently)

### Step 2: Identify Severity (S)

$$S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

where:
- $S = 1$: Negligible (no injury, no treatment)
- $S = 2$: Minor (first aid injury)
- $S = 3$: Moderate (lost-time injury)
- $S = 4$: Major (permanent disability, fatality avoided)
- $S = 5$: Catastrophic (single or multiple fatalities)

### Step 3: Compute Risk Score

$$R = L \times S$$

The risk score ranges from **1 (lowest)** to **25 (highest)**.

### Step 4: Apply the Risk Decision Matrix

$$\text{Action Level} = f(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if } 1 \le R \le 4 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 5 \le R \le 9 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 10 \le R \le 15 \\ \text{Extreme} & \text{if } 16 \le R \le 25 \end{cases}$$

### Step 5: Determine Control Strategy

| Risk Level | Action Required |
|------------|------------------|
| **Low** (R ≤ 4) | Monitor; no immediate action |
| **Medium** (5 ≤ R ≤ 9) | Reduce risk where practicable |
| **High** (10 ≤ R ≤ 15) | Reduce risk before commencement; senior management approval |
| **Extreme** (16 ≤ R ≤ 25) | **HALT work immediately**; redesign to eliminate risk |

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Noise Dose Calculation (Industrial Hygiene Application)

### Step 1: Define Reference Criterion

The OSHA action level is **85 dBA** (TWA over 8 hours), and the PEL is **90 dBA**.

### Step 2: Apply the Exchange Rate

OSHA uses a **5 dB exchange rate**, meaning each 5 dB increase doubles the noise dose:

$$T_{\text{allowed}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \text{ hours}$$

For example, at $L = 95$ dBA:
$$T_{\text{allowed}} = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^1} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Dose

$$\text{Dose (\%)} = \frac{C_{\text{measured}}}{T_{\text{allowed}}} \times 100$$

where $C_{\text{measured}}$ is the actual exposure time at that level.

### Step 4: Determine 8-hour TWA

$$\text{TWA} = 16.61 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{100}\right) + 90$$

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Application Scenario | Hazard Type | Recommended Tool | Risk Control | KPI to Track |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Chemical reactor overpressure | Process | HAZOP + LOPA | Relief valve, SIS | TRIR, FAR |
| Construction fall from height | Mechanical | Checklist + JSA | Guardrails, safety nets | Fall incident rate |
| Confined space entry (sewer) | Atmospheric | Permit-to-work | Gas testing, ventilation | Confined space incidents |
| Crane lifting near power line | Electrical | What-If Analysis | De-energization, barricade | Near-miss reports |
| Worker exposure to silica dust | Chronic Health | Industrial hygiene monitoring | Wet cutting, respirators | TWA, PEL compliance |
| Storage tank fire/explosion | Process/Physical | FTA + ETA | Foam system, dyke wall | Process incident rate |
| Office ergonomic injury | Ergonomic | Walk-through survey | Adjustable furniture, training | DART rate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Safety Engineering Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Industrial Process or Activity] --> node2[Step 1: Hazard Identification]
    node2 --> node3[Step 2: Risk Estimation]
    node3 --> node4[Step 3: Risk Evaluation via L x S Matrix]
    node4 --> node5{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    node5 -->|Yes| node6[Proceed with Controls]
    node5 -->|No| node7[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    node7 --> node8[1 Elimination]
    node7 --> node9[2 Substitution]
    node7 --> node10[3 Engineering Controls]
    node7 --> node11[4 Administrative Controls]
    node7 --> node12[5 PPE]
    node8 --> node13[Step 5: Implement and Monitor]
    node9 --> node13
    node10 --> node13
    node11 --> node13
    node12 --> node13
    node6 --> node13
    node13 --> node14[PDCA Continuous Improvement Loop]
    node14 --> node2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Application Mapping by Industry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Safety Engineering Principles] --> nodeB[Process Industry]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Construction]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Manufacturing]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Mining]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Oil and Gas]

    nodeB --> nodeG[HAZOP, LOPA, PSM, QRA]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Fall Protection, Scaffolding, PPE]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Machine Guarding, LOTO, ISO 45001]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Ventilation, Roof Bolting, Gas Monitoring]
    nodeF --> nodeK[ATEX, Permit to Work, ALARP, SMS]

    nodeG --> nodeL[Outcome: Reduced Process Incidents]
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Risk Matrix Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1[Hazard Identified] --> nodeR2[Assign L: 1 to 5]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Assign S: 1 to 5]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4[Compute R = L x S]
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5{R value?}
    nodeR5 -->|1 to 4| nodeR6[LOW: Accept, Monitor]
    nodeR5 -->|5 to 9| nodeR7[MEDIUM: Reduce if Practicable]
    nodeR5 -->|10 to 15| nodeR8[HIGH: Reduce before Work]
    nodeR5 -->|16 to 25| nodeR9[EXTREME: HALT and Redesign]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hazard Identification → Risk Estimation → Risk Evaluation → Control → Monitor** architecture shown above is a near-universal blueprint endorsed by ISO 45001:2018, OSHA 3990-03, and the U.K. HSE's HSG65 framework. Memorizing this sequence will earn easy KTU marks for any application-type question.

## 4.4 Schematic of the Hierarchy of Controls (as a Block Stack)

```mermaid

    nodeTop[1 ELIMINATION: Most Effective] --> nodeSub[2 SUBSTITUTION]
    nodeSub --> nodeEng[3 ENGINEERING CONTROLS]
    nodeEng --> nodeAdm[4 ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS]
    nodeAdm --> nodePPE[5 PPE: Last Resort]
    nodePPE --> nodeBase[Worker]

    style nodeTop fill:#006400,color:#ffffff
    style nodeSub fill:#228B22,color:#ffffff
    style nodeEng fill:#7CFC00,color:#000000
    style nodeAdm fill:#FFD700,color:#000000
    style nodePPE fill:#FF6347,color:#ffffff
    style nodeBase fill:#8B0000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Application" in safety engineering. Give two examples of safety engineering applications in industry. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Application in safety engineering refers to the practical implementation of safety principles, hazard identification techniques, risk assessment methodologies, and control measures in real industrial settings to prevent accidents, injuries, and occupational illnesses.

**Examples:**
